coredata.dc:title: Doping and defect engineering in carbon-based electrocatalysts for enhanced electrochemical CO2 reduction: From 0D to 3D materials

coredata.dc:description: The increasing atmospheric CO2 levels and the urgent need for sustainable energy solutions have driven research into electrochemical CO2 reduction. Carbon-based materials have received significant attention for their potential as electrocatalysts, yet their inert nature often limits their performance. Defect engineering and heteroatom doping have emerged as transformative approaches to overcome these limitations, enhancing both catalytic activity and Faradaic efficiency. This review systematically examines the role of these strategies across diverse carbon materials, including graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, and boron-doped diamond. Special attention is given to the incorporation of heteroatoms, such as nitrogen and boron, and the modulation of defect structures to optimize CO2 reduction pathways. By exploring the interplay between dopant type, defect density, and material dimensionality, we provide a comprehensive understanding of how tailored carbon-based electrocatalysts can drive advancements in sustainable electrochemical CO2 conversion. This work underscores the potential of defect-engineered and doped carbon materials to revolutionize the field of electrocatalysis, paving the way for innovative solutions to environmental and energy challenges.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of various methods for converting CO2 into fuels.

The number of publications related to CO2 reduction, indicating (a) diverse methodologies and (b) the utilization of carbon-based materials across various dimensionalities. Analytical data compiled from the “Web of Science”.
Diverse allotropes and forms of carbon-based materials spanning from zero to three dimensions.

(a) Basic electrochemical setup for CO2 reduction to hydrocarbons. (b) Schematic diagram illustrating the electric double layer (EDL) structure for hydrocarbon production using the ECRR and the EDL Model. CO₂ molecules chemisorb onto the metal electrode surface, forming four distinct configurations corresponding to the active sites. The schematic shows cations primarily in the diffusion layer and the Outer Helmholtz Layer (OHL), which is typical for solvated ions under electrostatic attraction. Under specific conditions, such as strong electrode-ion interactions or specific adsorption, cations can also enter the Inner Helmholtz Plane (IHP). Anions, though typically repelled from the negatively charged electrode, may interact with the electrode surface if specifically adsorbed. This representation is adapted from Refs. [89, 91] to conceptually illustrate the EDL structure.

Standard potentials for electrochemical CO2 reduction reactions (vs SHE) involving variable electron counts (2eˉ, 4eˉ, 6eˉ, 8eˉ, 12eˉ, 14eˉ, 16eˉ, 18eˉ) leading to the formation of diverse C1/C2/C2+ hydrocarbons and oxygenate reduction products.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing atmospheric CO2 levels and the urgent need for sustainable energy solutions have driven research into electrochemical CO2 reduction. Carbon-based materials have received significant attention for their potential as electrocatalysts, yet their inert nature often limits their performance. Defect engineering and heteroatom doping have emerged as transformative approaches to overcome these limitations, enhancing both catalytic activity and Faradaic efficiency. This review systematically examines the role of these strategies across diverse carbon materials, including graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, and boron-doped diamond. Special attention is given to the incorporation of heteroatoms, such as nitrogen and boron, and the modulation of defect structures to optimize CO2 reduction pathways. By exploring the interplay between dopant type, defect density, and material dimensionality, we provide a comprehensive understanding of how tailored carbon-based electrocatalysts can drive advancements in sustainable electrochemical CO2 conversion. This work underscores the potential of defect-engineered and doped carbon materials to revolutionize the field of electrocatalysis, paving the way for innovative solutions to environmental and energy challenges.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The escalating levels of atmospheric CO2, driven by extensive deforestation and the overreliance on fossil fuels, have surged from 372 ppm in April 1999 to an alarming peak of 425 ppm in April 2023, surpassing the safety limit of 350 ppm. This surge contributes to pressing environmental and health concerns, including climate change, global warming, air quality degradation, and ocean acidification [1,2]. The natural balance of the carbon cycle, sustained over millions of years through plant-mediated CO2 assimilation and oxygen release, has been disrupted by widespread deforestation to accommodate urbanization and population growth. Consequently, there is an urgent need to address the rising concentration of greenhouse gases in the atmosphere. Governments and researchers, cognizant of the challenges posed by depleting energy resources and environmental crises, have been actively exploring renewable energy sources such as wind, biopower, geothermal, hydropower, ocean, and solar power. Despite these efforts, only 11.2 % of global energy consumption is met by renewable energies [2,3].

In response to sustainability concerns, there is a growing emphasis on converting CO2 into value-added chemicals and fuels, aligning with a waste-to-wealth strategy. Various methods, as illustrated in Fig. 1 , involve transforming CO2 into C1/C2/C2+ hydrocarbons and oxygenate products [4–8]. These methods encompass thermo-[9–14], sono-[15,16], photo-[17–19], piezo-[20–23], piezo-photo-[24–26], piezo-electro-[27–29], bio-[30–36], bio-electro-[33,37,38], photo-bio-electro-[39], electro-[4,8,40–49], and photo-electro-[50] catalytic chemical reactions, utilizing either heterogeneous or homogeneous catalysts. Fig. 2a presents the number of journal publications on different CO2 reduction techniques as of January 2, 2024, obtained from the Web of Science website, while Fig. 2b categorizes articles based on different types and dimensions of carbon-based electrocatalyst materials. Photocatalytic CO2 reduction techniques dominate with 10,508 articles, followed by 7196 articles on electrochemical CO2 reduction. Notably, graphene-based materials are extensively used in CO2 reduction reactions, with carbon nanotubes ranking second, and zero-dimensional carbon-based materials having the least representation. This distribution is attributed to the simplicity and affordability of photocatalytic CO2 reduction techniques and the well-established synthesis methods for graphene-based materials in comparison to zero-dimensional carbon-based electrocatalysts.

While the “waste-to-wealth” vision of transforming CO2 into valuable fuels and chemicals holds immense promise, the path towards robust and highly efficient CO2-to-fuel conversion systems is fraught with obstacles, hindering their large-scale industrialization. These challenges stem from CO2's inherent stability, characterized by its non-polarity, centrosymmetric configuration, and strong carbon‑oxygen double bonds, leading to a formidable CO bond dissociation energy of ∼806 kJmol−1 – significantly exceeding that of typical CH, CC, or CO bonds [30,43,51,52]. Furthermore, CO2's chemical inertness, stemming from its large HOMO-LUMO gap, and weak adsorption capacity on catalyst surfaces further impede efficient activation and conversion. Adding to the complexity, CO2 dissociation into reactive intermediates is inherently thermodynamically unfavorable, requiring significant energy input, rendering the reduction process an uphill battle. Despite these formidable challenges, electrochemical CO2 reduction (ECRR) emerges as a particularly promising contender, offering several compelling advantages. Firstly, ECRR directly converts CO2 into value-added chemicals and fuels, promoting resource utilization and sustainability. Secondly, it operates under mild reaction conditions, at ambient temperature and pressure, minimizing energy consumption. Thirdly, by fine-tuning parameters like applied current, voltage, and catalyst choice, ECRR allows for precise control over reaction rates and product selectivity, enabling targeted product formation. Fourthly, ECRR readily integrates with renewable energy sources like wind, hydro, or solar power, paving the way for a carbon-neutral energy cycle. Finally, the compact and modular nature of ECRR reactor systems facilitates potential upscaling for industrial applications [8,44,53–57].

In the realm of electrochemical CO2 reduction (ECRR), the final product hinges on a delicate interplay of factors, including applied potential, electrolyte composition, electrode material, temperature, and pH. [3,47,58–64] The electrode material's composition, conductivity, and chemical properties, such as functional groups and surface properties like porosity and active sites, play a crucial role in activity, selectivity, and efficiency [3,47,58–64]. Y. Hori categorized metal electrodes into four groups based on their CO₂ reduction product selectivity: (1) In, Pb, Sn, and Cd predominantly produce HCOO− or HCOOH; (2) Au, Ag, Zn, and P favor CO generation; (3) Cu can facilitate the production of hydrocarbons and multi‑carbon compounds; and (4) Ni, Fe, Pt, and Ti exhibit negligible activity for CO₂ reduction reactions [65].

Commonly used electrode materials fall into categories such as metallic materials from nanoparticle to bulk structures (Cu, Ni, Pt, Zn, Au, Ag, Sn, Ti, Cr, Hg, Pb, Fe), metal-based materials (oxides/sulphides/hydrides), metal-based molecular complexes (metal-organic frameworks), single-atom catalysts, and carbon-based materials. In particular, carbon-based materials with different dimensionalities (0D, 1D, 2D, 3D) have gained widespread attention. As shown in Fig. 3 , carbon-based materials are versatile players appearing in a multitude of dimensions, from 0D carbon dots and fullerenes to the elongated elegance of 1D carbon nanotubes and nanofibers, the ultrathin layers of 2D graphene and graphyne, and the robust structures of 3D diamond, graphite, nanodiamond, porous carbon, and carbon black. Carbonaceous materials, despite their chemical inertness in aqueous solutions under negative potentials, exhibit a higher onset potential for hydrogen (H₂) evolution compared to metal electrodes. This higher onset potential suppresses H₂ generation, thereby favoring CO₂ reduction reactions. The diverse allotropes of carbon, characterized by varying orbital hybridizations (sp, sp2, sp3), chirality, structural arrangements, and dimensions, possess unique properties such as exceptional mechanical strength, chemical stability, electrical conductivity, and porous surface characteristics. These attributes make carbon-based materials highly versatile for applications in sensors, water splitting, solar cells, energy conversion, and energy storage. Their unique properties, combined with the ability to enhance activity and selectivity for ECRR through heteroatom incorporation or defect engineering, establish them as promising candidates for ECRR compared to other electrocatalyst materials [8,51,66–73].

Carbon-based electrocatalyst materials are highly suitable for ECRR applications due to their exceptional properties and versatile tunability. Renowned for their high surface area, inherent porosity, and abundance of defects, these materials provide a dynamic platform for catalytic optimization. Defects in carbon-based materials can be classified by dimensionality: (a) zero-dimensional defects such as point defects, including interstitials, vacancies, substitutions, and heteroatom doping; (b) one-dimensional defects like dislocations and line defects; (c) two-dimensional defects, including planar features like grain or phase boundaries; and (d) three-dimensional defects, such as voids, precipitates, or volume irregularities. Both intrinsic defects (e.g., vacancies, dislocations, edge sites, grain boundaries) and extrinsic defects (e.g., heteroatom doping, functional groups) can be intentionally introduced during synthesis or post-treatment processes. These defects modulate structural, electronic, chemical, mechanical, and optical properties by altering the distribution of electrons within the material. Defects not only enhance the electrochemical properties of carbon-based materials but also serve as active sites for reactions and electron trapping, thereby significantly improving their performance as electrocatalysts for ECRR. Additionally, the unique electronic, chemical, and electrochemical properties of carbon-based materials stem from the diverse arrangements of carbon atoms in sp3 (tetrahedral), sp2 (trigonal), and sp (linear) hybridized states, resulting in a range of π and σ bond configurations. Intrinsic defects in carbon layers, characterized by non-sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, act as electroactive sites due to the locally enhanced reactivity of the π-electron-dominated system. This distinct combination of properties positions carbon-based materials as highly effective and tunable candidates for ECRR.

Compared to pristine carbon structures, which are largely inert in electrochemical applications, defect-rich carbon structures offer enhanced functionality by modulating electronic properties, increasing exposed active sites for redox reactions, and providing ideal docking sites for heteroatom dopants. While the term “defect” traditionally implies a material imperfection, researchers have demonstrated the positive impact of defects on tuning material properties and enhancing catalytic performance, leading to a surge in related studies [57,73–79]. Intrinsic structural defects in carbon-based materials can be classified into four main types: vacancies/holes, topological defects, edge defects, and distortions/curvatures. Topological defects involve geometric distortions in the carbon lattice, where pristine hexagonal structures are transformed into unconventional polygonal arrangements, such as 585 defects (one octagon and two pentagons), Stone-Wales defects (5775 defects, comprising pentagon–heptagon pairs), and isolated pentagons. These defects alter electron distribution asymmetrically, introducing high chemical reactivity or catalytic activity near the non-hexagonal polygons. Edge defects are prevalent in two-dimensional carbon materials like graphene nanoribbons and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, arising from structural imperfections at the edges. Common configurations include armchair and zigzag edges, along with less typical forms such as K-region, cove, fjord, bay, and gulf edges. These edge defects leave carbon atoms coordinatively unsaturated with dangling bonds, resulting in high-energy states prone to chemical reactions, bonding, and reconstructions. Edge defects also occur near vacancies or holes, where missing atoms leave unsaturated carbon atoms exposed. Vacancies, the most ubiquitous point defects, arise from the removal of atoms from the crystal lattice. Single or multiple vacancies can coalesce to form larger voids or holes, inducing reactivity and structural instability. The resulting vacancies often lead to topological defects as the lattice restructures to achieve lower energy states. Distortions or curvatures emerge from localized strain, causing CC bond lengthening or shortening. This introduces asymmetric charge density distributions, further influencing the material's catalytic properties [79–81]. F. Pan et al. [82] demonstrated that the CO adsorption energy at zigzag and armchair edge sites is approximately 0.3 eV weaker than that at bulk sites in graphene nanoribbons and carbon nanotubes (CNTs). This reduction in adsorption energy facilitates easier CO desorption from edge sites compared to basal plane sites, highlighting the enhanced reactivity of edge defects. While intrinsic defects in carbon-based materials improve their electrocatalytic performance compared to pristine, chemically inert counterparts, the activity remains limited. In contrast, carbon-based materials with extrinsic defects exhibit remarkable electrocatalytic activity for CO2 reduction reactions. By introducing heteroatoms or functional groups into the carbon skeleton, new carbon-heteroatom bonds are formed, which significantly alter the electronic structure, modulate local chemical reactivity, and enhance the overall electrocatalytic performance. The incorporation of electronegative elements or species induces intramolecular charge transfer, creating highly electroactive sites. This not only enhances conductivity but also improves catalytic reactivity, making extrinsically defect-engineered carbon materials highly promising for ECRR applications [80].

Within the context of ECRR, strategic defect engineering and heteroatom doping emerge as powerful tools for optimizing the electrode surface and tailoring its interaction with the electrolyte. By fine-tuning the electronic structure and charge distribution of the carbon lattice, these techniques offer exquisite control over chemisorption properties and ultimately, CO2 reduction performance. In essence, intrinsic defects like holes, vacancies, grain boundaries, dislocations, topological and edge sites, alongside extrinsic modifications via heteroatom doping (metal/non-metal), provide a playground for manipulating electronic structure, electron density, interlayer spacing, and charge delocalization, all with the aim of enhancing electrocatalytic activity [56,57,67,70,73,83,84]. Defects in carbon-based materials play a crucial role in modulating charge distribution around active sites, thereby enhancing their catalytic properties. Additionally, these defects provide stabilization for dopant species, forming coordinated motifs that serve as highly effective catalytic active sites. As a result, defect engineering and heteroatom doping have been intensively studied in recent years as strategies to enhance the performance of carbon-based electrocatalysts.

The prominent role of defects in carbon-based ECRR catalysts hinges on their intimate relationship with the electrode surface, the very stage where the transformation of CO2 unfolds [84]. The presence of diverse defects, both intrinsic and extrinsic, creates a dynamic landscape of active sites for CO2 molecule adsorption and subsequent reduction reactions. This intricate choreography is further facilitated by the exceptionally large surface area and low onset potential of carbon-based electrocatalysts, maximizing the interaction probability between CO2 molecules/ reaction intermediates and reactive sites of catalyst. The combined effect of these factors significantly enhances catalyst activity and selectivity, paving the way for efficient CO2 conversion to fuels [80,85]. Therefore, defect engineering and heteroatom doping have been recognized as powerful tools in tailoring the performance of carbon-based ECRR catalysts.

In recent years, extensive research has been conducted on optimizing carbon-based materials by manipulating their atomic and electronic structures through heteroatom doping and defect engineering. These strategies have become pivotal in designing advanced functional materials for diverse applications, including energy storage, conversion, and electrocatalysis. However, while many reviews exist on the electrochemical applications of carbon materials, there remains a significant gap in the literature addressing the synergistic interplay between doping and defect engineering, particularly in the context of ECRR. This review aims to fill this gap by providing an up-to-date and focused analysis of defect engineering strategies and their combined effects with doping on catalytic performance. To advance the development of high-performance electrocatalyst materials, this review critically examines key aspects of defect engineering: (a) identifying defect types, assessing defect density, and understanding the reactivity of defect sites; (b) elucidating the roles of specific defect types in reaction pathways; (c) establishing the relationship between defect structures and electrocatalytic performance; and (d) optimizing defect density to achieve maximum catalytic efficiency. We categorize intrinsic and extrinsic defects across dimensional structures, ranging from 0D to 3D, and emphasize extrinsic defect engineering strategies such as heteroatom doping and composite formation. These approaches enable significant modifications to the physicochemical and electronic properties of carbon-based materials, enhancing CO2 adsorption, charge transfer, product selectivity, and overall catalytic activity. This review also examines the effects of dopant concentration and the microenvironment on the catalytic efficiency of defective or heteroatom-doped carbon materials, providing a detailed comparison of their performance. By unraveling the multifaceted relationships between defects, doping, and catalytic activity, we aim to provide a comprehensive framework for the rational design of next-generation, sustainable ECRR catalysts.

Electrocatalysis, a branch of electrochemistry utilizing a catalyst to enhance chemical reactions and interconvert chemical and electrical energy, involves oxidation and reduction reactions occurring at electrode surfaces in two separate compartments/half-cells, which are divided by a semi-permeable membrane. This occurs through the transfer of electrons between the electrocatalyst or electrode and the reactants. The electron transfer in electrocatalysis is facilitated by an externally applied potential through an electrical circuit, coupled with an ion-conducting phase electrolyte, maintaining charge neutrality and converting electrical energy into chemical energy through the generation of reaction products [7,43,49,55,63,65,71,86,87]. In the ECRR, the reduction of CO2 occurs at the cathode, while oxygen evolution takes place at the anode, as illustrated in Fig. 4a. To prevent contamination between the electrodes and maintain stable operation, an ion exchange membrane, such as a Nafion film, is used to separate the cathode and anode compartments effectively. In this review, the focus is on the cathode-electrolyte interface as the site of CO2 reduction, emphasizing a detailed investigation into the impact of cathodic materials on reduction efficiencies.

The Electrochemical CO2 Reduction Reaction (ECRR), involving multi-proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) reactions, takes place in various electrolytes such as ionic liquids, organic solvents, and aqueous mediums. At the nanoscale space of the electrode-electrolyte interface where electrochemical reactions occur, the electrical and chemical properties of this interface or reaction microenvironment become the determining factor for rate and selectivity. An electrical double layer, a region of charge accumulation, forms at the interface between the electrode and the electrolyte. This arises due to the interaction between the charged electrode surface and the ions in the electrolyte, influenced by the applied electric potential. When the electrode surfaces come into contact with the electrolyte, positive or negative charges accumulate within a thin layer (< 0.1 Å) of the anode or cathode surface under the influence of the applied voltage [88]. This charge accumulation is counterbalanced by the anions and cations of the electrolyte, driven by Coulomb forces as depicted in Fig. 4b. The interfacial space between the electrode and the electrolyte comprises five continuous regions: (i) Electrode surface, (ii) Inner Helmholtz plane (IHP), (iii) Outer Helmholtz plane (OHP), (iv) Diffuse layer, and (v) Bulk solution. In ECRR, the CO2 Reduction Reaction (CO2RR) predominantly occurs on the negatively charged cathode surface. The electrocatalytic reduction reactions generally take place at the IHP, the layer closest to the electrode surface, which is formed by adsorbed reactants, reaction intermediates (in the case of ECRR, CO2 reduction reaction intermediates), products, solvated electrolyte ions, specifically adsorbed ions, and solvent molecules exhibiting a strong chemical interaction with the electrode surface. The OHP, oppositely charged to the electrode surface, consists of solvated ions bound by long-range electrostatic attraction with the electrode. In the case of ECRR, due to the applied negative voltage, the cathode surface becomes negatively charged due to the accumulation of electrons. In response to this, cations accumulate near the electrode surface, forming a local electric field between the metal surface and the cationic wall [43,89–91]. The outermost diffuse layer follows the OHP, where ions are arranged disorderly, and its concentration decreases moving towards the bulk electrolyte, showing less effect on ECRR. Thus, the Helmholtz plane and the diffuse plane together form the electric double layer (EDL), behaving similarly to a capacitor in concentrated electrolyte conditions due to the interactions between the electrodes, reactants, and electrolytes [88,91]. The distribution and positioning of cations within the EDL are influenced by various factors, including the cation type, size, degree of hydration, externally applied potential, and the nature of the adsorbent. These factors collectively contribute to what is known as the cation effect, which directly impacts the EDL properties, leading to variations in current density and Faradaic efficiency [89,91].

Considering that ECRR takes place at the electrode surface, any alteration in the quantity and composition of adsorbates, specifically polar CO2RR intermediates, has a profound impact on the chemical and electronic properties of the electrocatalyst. This influence extends to the local electric field strength and subsequently shapes the structure of the EDL. Consequently, these changes significantly affect the selectivity and activity of the CO2RR. Furthermore, modifications in the concentration, composition, configuration, and bond arrangement of CO2RR intermediates not only impact the EDL, active site quantities, and adsorption energies but also influence and modulate the kinetics of the PCET at various atomic sites. This intricate interplay results in the production of diverse final reduction products [89,90]. Apart from the microenvironmental effects on the ECRR, a significant impact is observed due to variations in the chemical potential drop, reactivity, conductivity, and surface properties of the electrode material, which differ based on the material used. As a result, the surface charge magnitude varies, leading to alterations in the double-layer structure and influencing the absorbance of reaction intermediates/reactants and chemical reactions. This modification in the reaction rate, arising from the varied double-layer structure, is referred to as the secondary effect or the ‘Frumkin effect’ [88]. In a broader context, CO2RR leads to the formation of variable final C1/C2/C2+ hydrocarbons and oxygenate products based on the number of electrons involved in CO2 reduction reaction pathways (2eˉ, 4eˉ, 6eˉ, 8eˉ, 12eˉ, 14eˉ, 16eˉ, 18eˉ). Fig. 5 illustrates the standard reduction potential for generating C1, C2, and C3 products with the corresponding number of electrons (eˉ) required. Table 1 summarizes CO₂ electroreduction pathways with varying electron counts (2e− to 18e−) and their standard potentials. Consequently, the deliberate tuning of cathode material composition, surface area, active sites, and structures becomes pivotal in effectively modulating the reaction kinetics for achieving high selectivity towards desired products during ECRR at the electrode surface by optimizing the microenvironment parameters. Over the past decades, researchers and scientists have extensively explored various electrode materials to obtain specific desired products. In recent years, carbon-based electrocatalysts have attracted significant attention, with different carbon allotropes exhibiting distinct chemisorption capacities and selectivity towards CO2RR intermediates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Understanding the mechanism behind ECRR product formation
ce:section.ce:para: In a standard ECRR, the process unfolds in the cathodic half-cell of the electrochemical set-up, serving as the working electrode where CO2 gas is introduced. The other two electrodes in the system include the counter electrode or anode, where the oxygen evolution reaction transpires due to the oxidation of water, and the third reference electrode which provides a stable reference potential unaffected by the environment for the electrochemical setup. The ECRR comprises three fundamental stages: (i) adsorption of CO2 molecules onto the cathode surface through the active sites, (ii) proton migration or electron transfer leading to the cleavage of CO bonds, resulting in the formation of CH or CC bonds and reaction intermediates, and (iii) desorption of product molecules from the electrode surface. In ECRR, the generation of the *CO2 •ˉ intermediate through an electron transfer is considered to be the decisive step of the reaction. The performance of an electrocatalyst in ECRR is determined by several critical parameters, including: (i) Onset potential - the minimum potential applied in the system to initiate a specific CO2 reduction product, (ii) Overpotential - the difference between the thermodynamic potential and the applied potential, (iii) Current density or partial current density - the total current divided by the electrode's surface area, (iv) Energetic efficiency - the overall energy utilization rate, (v) Tafel slope or Tafel plot - where the Tafel plot illustrates the relationship between the electrochemical reaction rate and the overpotential of an electrode, while the Tafel slope indicates the electrochemical reaction rate, (vi) Faradaic efficiency or selectivity - a measure of the fraction of the total current consumed to produce a specific product, and (vii) Turnover frequency - the number of molecules reacting per active site per unit time, reflecting the reactivity or yield of a certain product obtained using a unit catalyst [56,77,92,93].

As CO2 is a thermodynamically stable molecule with strong CO bonds, inducing the CO2 reduction reaction demands substantial energy input. The ECRR unfolds through multi-electron and proton transfer reactions, yielding a spectrum of reaction products including hydrocarbons and oxygenates classified based on the number of carbon atoms in the molecules - C1, C2, C2+ products. Kinetically, the formation of alcohols and hydrocarbons necessitates more energy than that of HCOOH and CO, i.e., high overpotential due to the greater number of electrons required for product formation.

In ECRR, the activation of the CO2 molecule involves weakening the CO bond by altering the linear structure to a bent configuration with electron redistributions. This occurs through three distinct pathways based on the chemisorbed bonding directions of the C atom of CO2 to the catalyst surface: (i) single electron transfer when C of CO2 is bonded to the catalyst, (ii) proton-coupled electron transfer when C of CO2 is bonded to the catalyst, and (iii) proton-coupled electron transfer when O atoms are connected to the catalyst [8,43,52,94]. In general, ECRR is initiated by an electron transfer to form *CO2 •ˉ at −1.9 V vs SHE, which is considered the rate-limiting step. The formed *CO2 •ˉ anion radical is unstable and readily protonated, forming *COOH or *OCHO intermediate. The further dissociation and reduction of *COOH yield *CO intermediate and H2O. The generation of *CO intermediate is crucial and serves as a common reaction intermediate for various products, as its adsorption energy to the catalyst surface determines the selectivity of the C1, C2, or C2+ ECRR products. The Sabatier principle comes into play, stating that the binding energy between the catalyst's active sites and key intermediates or reactants should be neither too weak nor too strong. If the bond between the catalyst and the reactant is too weak, there will be minimal interaction, hindering effective catalysis. Conversely, an excessively strong bond can obstruct the desorption of reaction intermediates from the catalyst surface, thereby inhibiting subsequent reactions. It indicates the critical importance of selecting an appropriate electrocatalyst tailored for the desired reduction product or optimizing catalyst properties for enhanced performance. An ideal electrocatalyst should effectively facilitate CO2 activation and the formation of *CO intermediates to promote CC coupling. To prevent *CO desorption from the catalyst surface and the production of CO product, the catalyst should also have an optimal *CO binding energy. Catalysts with strong *CO adsorption energy might be poisoned in the ECRR process as the electrode surface becomes covered with *CO, limiting product desorption. Alternatively, a high density of *CO intermediates at the catalyst surface can promote CC coupling by increasing intermediate contact. The strong adsorption energy of *CO has the potential to induce poisoning on the electrode surface. This phenomenon kinetically suppresses the CO2 reduction, subsequently entering into competition with the hydrogen evolution reaction, which is responsible for generating H2 as the primary product [8,66,93–95]. Fig. 6a illustrates the pathways for generating various C1, C2, C2+ products through different CO2 activations with various chemisorbed configurations between the CO2 molecule and the catalyst surface and electron or PCET mechanisms. This figure also elucidates various pathways for product formation through the creation of variable CO2 reduction reaction intermediates and products.

In ECRR, the generation of C1 products such as CO, HCHO, HCOOH, CH4, and CH3OH involves a crucial step: the formation of *CO intermediates resulting from the dissociation of *COOH. This step is particularly vital as *CO serves as the common intermediate for the aforementioned C1 products. Notably, the formation of *CO is recognized as the rate-determining step, requiring overcoming a high binding energy to form *CHO or *COH through protonation. For HCOOH, *OCHO has been proposed as the reduction reaction intermediate, formed either through PCET of CO2 (CO2 + e − + H + → ∗ OCHO) or direct protonation of CO2 (CO2 + e − → ∗ OCHO) [7,11,98]. Formate (HCOOH) as a CO2 reduction product can be generated through two primary pathways: (i) ∗ +

[97] identified four distinct pathways for formate/formic acid production, categorized based on the attachment orientation of intermediates. These include the monodentate intermediate route (Fig. 6b), the surface-bound carbonate intermediate route (Fig. 6c), and two variations of the CO2 − radical intermediate route (Fig. 6d, e). In the monodentate or bidentate intermediate routes (Fig. 6), the intermediates bind to metal‑hydrogen-bonded electrode surfaces — formed by the direct protonation of the electrode with H+ — via one or two oxygen atoms, respectively. In contrast, for the CO2 − radical intermediate route, CO2 binds to the metal electrode surface through the formation of a metal‑carbon bond. Subsequently, the oxygen atom in the CO2 − radical undergoes protonation, leading to the reduction of CO2 to HCOOH or, occasionally, CO and H2O [97,99,100].

Beyond C1 products, the synthesis of C2+ products, such as C2H4, CH3CH2OH, from CO2 is desirable due to their high-energy value. However, the reduction reaction pathways for these products face thermodynamic and kinetic challenges. The activation of CO2 to achieve CC coupling poses a significant thermodynamic barrier, and the process is kinetically unfavorable as it necessitates multielectron-coupled proton transfer. The production of C2+ products can be achieved by three distinct pathways: (i) the CO insertion mechanism, reducing *COH to *CH2 intermediates with the addition of CO, leading to the formation of CH3COOH, (ii) the dimerization of two *CO forming *COCO species, subsequently protonated to form *COCOH, and (iii) the direct combination of *COH and CO forming *COCOH, then undergoing multiple PCET processes to generate C2+ products. The formation of the *CH2CHO intermediate is a critical step in producing C2H4 and CH3CH2OH as the final reduction products. Among the C2+ products, Cu-based electrodes exhibit heightened selectivity in generating C2H4 through hydrogenolysis of *CH2CHO compared to other oxygenates, particularly *CH3CHO, which is formed via the hydrogenation of *CH2CHO and yields C2H5OH. This preference for C2H4 production on Cu-based electrodes can be attributed to the 0.2 eV lower energy barrier for its reduction, in contrast to the formation of C2H5OH from *CH3CHO. Consequently, the intricate network of CO2 reduction reaction products and their respective selectivities is intricately influenced by the formation of distinct reaction intermediates through diverse pathways. The choice of electrocatalyst material, the energy barriers associated with specific product formations, the localized microenvironment, and the applied potential in electrocatalytic reactions collectively influence the selection of these pathways. Therefore, careful consideration is imperative when selecting the electrocatalyst and optimizing reaction parameters to achieve superior performance and selectivity [4,7,52,53,55,84,101–103].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of heteroatom doping and defect engineering in ECRR
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based materials exhibit remarkable mechanical and chemical stability, high electrical conductivity, porosity, environmental friendliness, and cost-effectiveness. Additionally, their diverse allotropes showcase various forms of carbon orbital hybridization and contain inherent defects. These attributes position carbon-based materials as promising candidates for electrocatalytic CO2 reduction reactions. However, pristine carbon materials are inherently inactive for CO2 reduction due to their electroneutral nature, resulting in limited CO2 adsorption capacity. To overcome this limitation, heteroatoms, both non-metal (B [104–109], N [105,110–115], S [116–118], F [119–122], P [123–125]) and metal (Zn [126–128], Cu [128–135], Co [136–138], Fe [82,139–141], Ni [142–145], Au [134,146–149], Ag [150–152], Pd [153], Sn [154]), as well as semiconductor oxides/sulphides (SnO2 [107,155–158], MoS2 [159,160], Cu2O [131,161–163], CuS [164]), molecular complexes [165–167], and single-atom catalysts [82,126,128,139,168,169], are incorporated into carbon-based materials.

These heteroatom doping and composite formation alter the charge distribution, effectively breaking the electroneutrality of carbon, and enhance CO2 adsorption and activation capacities. Intrinsic defects in carbon-based materials, mainly found in topologies and edges (zigzag and armchair edges), introduce numerous unpaired π electrons and expedite electron transfer. Fig. 7 illustrates different types of defects and doping in carbon-based materials. These defects reduce the binding energies required for the formation of key intermediates, offering a high concentration of active sites. Due to the high charge densities at the edge carbon atoms, these defects also serve as active sites for reactions. This scenario of defect formation and heteroatom doping alters reaction pathways, as porous, high surface area carbon materials provide an abundance of active sites, a prerequisite for accessing CO2 molecules, mass transfer, and charge transfer [57,80,83,93,94]. Consequently, this influences product selectivity and activity, as different binding energies of CO2 intermediates are formed. Thus, optimizing the properties of carbon-based material catalyst by defect engineering strategy can significantly enhance Faradaic efficiency, product selectivity, and current density.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic CO2 reduction using carbon-based materials
Electrocatalytic CO2 reduction in zero-dimensional carbon-based materials
ce:section.ce:para: Zero-dimensional carbon-based materials encompass various structures, including fullerenes, carbon dots, nanodiamonds, graphene quantum dots, and onion-like carbon structures. Among these, fullerenes exist in different configurations with different carbon atoms such as C70, C76, C82, and C84, with C60 being the most extensively studied. C60, characterized by a closed hollow cage structure formed by the linkage of 60 sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, features 12 pentagons and hexagons, resulting in a simple spherical symmetric structure with an external diameter of 0.71 nm. The unique electronic structure on the surface of C60, where the π orbital extends beyond the cage, contributes to high electron affinity and mobility, making it well-suited for electrocatalytic processes. Despite possessing distinctive electrical and optical properties, the catalytic activities of fullerenes are enhanced through heteroatom doping. Introducing heteroatoms with varying electronegativities compared to carbon alters electronic interactions, creating active centers that boost catalytic performance [170].

Z. Chen et al. [171] synthesized N-doped fullerene derivatives (NC61) through the high-temperature pyrolysis of pristine [6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PC61BM) and urea under an argon atmosphere for the electrochemical reduction of CO2 to formate. In comparison to pristine C61, N-C61–800 (NC61 heated at 800 °C) exhibited a higher current density and a more positive onset reduction potential of −0.42 V, compared to −0.63 V for C61 in CO2-saturated 0.5 M KHCO3 electrolyte. Among the six different NC61 samples prepared with various heating temperatures (N-C61–500, N-C61–600, N-C61–700, N-C61–800, N-C61–900, and N-C61–1000), N-C61–800 demonstrated the highest formate Faradaic efficiency of 91.2 %, at a moderate overpotential of 0.7 V and a partial current density of 12.2 mA cm−2. Apart from H2 resulting from water electrolysis, no other gas products were detected for both NC61 and pristine C61. The enhanced ECRR of N-C61–800 was attributed to the synergetic effect of greater graphitic N-defects, identified as the main active sites for formate formation, and a larger electrochemically active surface area. Here the formate was formed through the generation of a *CO2 •ˉ radical intermediate via single-electron reduction of CO2 (CO2 + e − → CO2 •ˉ), followed by PCET involving a proton from HCO3ˉ in the electrolyte. This reaction is facilitated by the smaller pK a of HCO3ˉ, the electrolyte (CO2 •ˉ + HCO3ˉ + e − → HCOO− + CO3 2ˉ) [171]. The ECRR activity and selectivity were improved by downsizing the C60 fullerene structure to sub-nano levels through fragmentation via heating and plasma treatment. D. Yan et al. [172] studied a comprehensive approach to fragment C60, involving both plasma treatment and heating under Ar and N2 gas at 900 °C. This method resulted in the creation of more active centers and introduced N and O doping through a surface curvature and defect engineering strategy. Four nitrogen species, namely N oxide, graphitic N, pyridinic N, and pyrrolic N, originating from N dopings, were exclusively identified in the C60-N2-plasma-900 sample. These nitrogen species played pivotal roles in facilitating the electrocatalytic CO2 reduction to CO. In contrast, no discernible electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction activities were observed in the pristine C60, C60 (Ar-plasma), and C60 (N2-plasma) samples, with H2 being the primary product. However, the C60-N2-plasma-900 electrode exhibited the highest FE of 96.8 % for CO production at −0.6 V vs RHE, accompanied by a partial current density of 1.8 mA cm−2 at −0.8 V vs RHE. The synergistic effect of N/O doping and C60 structure fragmentation induced by plasma heating played a pivotal role in enhancing ECRR performance. This enhancement was achieved by modifying the electronic structure and distributions, thereby promoting the formation of COOH* intermediates [172].

B. Zhao et al. [173] employed C60 as an electronic buffer to stabilize the Cu+ site, thereby constructing in situ Cu0–Cu+ double-active sites crucial for the preferential generation of C2+ products (C2H4 and C2H5OH) over C1 products (CO and HCOOH) through CC coupling pathways. As depicted in Fig. 8a, the formation of Cu0–Cu+ double-active sites occurred through electroreduction during the ECRR process on the CuO-C60 electrode surface, allowing electron transfer from Cu0 to C60, with C60 acting as the electronic buffer. This facilitated the production of C2+ products with a notable FE of 61 % (Fig. 8f) at a partial current density of 366 mA cm−2 in a broad potential window of −1.6 V vs RHE, while effectively suppressing H2 production. The significance of Cu0–Cu+ double-active site formation in the presence of C60 (CuO-C60) for product selectivity was evident from the maximized H2 evolution observed in the CuO electrode. The electrocatalytic reduction of CO2 on CuO-C60 evidenced the formation of *CHO and coupled *CO–CHO intermediates, confirming the preferential coupling of *CHO and *CO over *CO dimerization on the Cu0–Cu+ double-active sites due to the lower energy barrier of coupling *CHO and *CO than that of *CO dimerization. The free energy diagram for *CHO formation and CC coupling over the Cu species with C60 is illustrated in Fig. 8 (b, c) [173]. In another study, fullerenes (C60 and C70) were incorporated into the pore-channels of a Co-porphyrin-based MOF (C60@MOF-545-Co, C70@MOF-545-Co) using a one-pot impregnation method, as depicted in Fig. 8d [174]. In this composite catalyst, the π-electron system of the fullerene appeared to overlap with the π-electron system of the porphyrin in MOF, facilitating high electron transfer capabilities and CO2 adsorption due to an increased number of active centers, thereby enhancing ECRR selectivity. With the impregnation of C60 into the MOF-545-Co catalyst, the electrochemically active surface area was increased by 2 times, from 275 cm2 to 417.5 cm2. These collectively favorable properties induced by the introduction of fullerene resulted in an increased FE of CO2 reduction to CO, with percentages as follows: C60 (11.5 %), MOF-545-Co (70.2 %), C60@MOF-545-Co (97.0 %, −0.7 V, 37 mA cm−2), C70@MOF-545-Co (91.1 %, −0.8 V, 35.6 mA cm−2). A comparative graph of C60@MOF-545-Co, MOF-545-Co, C60@MOF-545, and C60 is presented in Fig. 8g. Additionally, Fig. 8h illustrates the change in FE of C60@MOF-545-Co with different C60 loading capacities [174].

In addition to fullerenes, carbon dots (Cdots) represent a distinct type of carbon allotrope, featuring quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with diameters ranging from 2 to 10 nm. These particles primarily consist of a mixture of sp2- and sp3-hybridized carbon atoms and possess a significant number of exposed edge sites. Carbon dots, distinguished by their high oxygen content and greater number of graphenic layers compared to graphene dots, possess numerous exposed edge sites that exhibit distinctive electronic properties. These sites act as efficient electron donors or acceptors, regulating electron distribution and transport behavior. Due to these exceptional surface and electrical characteristics, Cdots serve as highly effective electron sinks in ECRR, thereby significantly enhancing both the selectivity and activity of the process [176].

Cdots-based materials can be stabilized through various surface functionalization techniques, which play a pivotal role in modulating their activity and effectiveness in ECRR. Y. Zhou et al. [175] functionalized Cdots with -NH2 (named as NCD) and incorporated them into the Cu/CuO catalyst, resulting in the formation of the NCDs/Cu/CuO composite catalyst. The introduction of amino-functionalized Cdots i.e., NCDs increased the overall electron transfer rate between Cu/CuO particles by 37 %, thereby reducing the electron transfer resistance. As shown in Fig. 8e, acting as an electron sink, the amino-modified Cdots augmented the concentration of electrons on the catalyst surface and increased CO2 adsorption capacities by 18.2 %. This enhancement significantly promoted CC couplings, facilitating the selective conversion of CO2 to the multi‑carbon reduction product, C2H4. Furthermore, the binding energy of Cu2+ was strengthened upon the addition of amino-modified Cdots, resulting in a higher concentration of electrons at the composite interfaces and inducing multi-electron reduction reactions. The introduction of Cdots to the Cu/CuO catalyst increased the Faradaic efficiency of C2H4 generation from 47.2 % to 50.3 %. With the incorporation of amino-modified Cdots, the FE further increased to 56.6 % (Fig. 8i). Additionally, the selectivity for CO decreased from 27.2 % to 11.1 %, while the selectivity for C2H4 increased from 69.4 % to 84.5 %. This enhancement can be attributed to the role of NCDs as electron sinks, their ability to increase the electron transfer rate, and their improved CO₂ adsorption capacity due to amino functionalization [175].

The noteworthy impact of Cdots on enhancing electron transfer and electrocatalytic CO2 reduction activity has also been investigated by K. Lv et al. [159]. In this study, N-doped Cdots were introduced onto the N-doped MoS2 surface through a solvothermal process. The incorporation of N-doped Cdots onto the MoS2 catalyst surface provided several beneficial effects, including improved electrical conductivity, enhanced electron transportation, and a reduction in the onset potential. Through N-doping in MoS2, the energy barrier for COOH* intermediate formation decreased from 0.87 eV to 0.76 eV, leading to an increased generation of electrons on the Mo edge atom, thereby enhancing CO2 reduction to CO. The CO2 reduction mechanism on the N-MoS2 electrode surface is illustrated in Fig. 9a. N-MoS2 was prepared by removing NCDs from the N-MoS2@NCDs-180 sample through heating in the muffle furnace at 300 °C for 12 h. The free energy diagrams for CO2 reduction to CO on the MoS2 and N-MoS2 electrodes are presented in Fig. 9b. The introduction of N-Cdots resulted in a substantial improvement in the Faradaic efficiency of CO production by MoS2, increasing from 41.7 % at −0.95 V to 90.2 % at −0.9 V. Furthermore, the partial current density showed an increase from 26.7 mA cm−2 for N-MoS2 to 36.2 mA cm−2 for N-MoS2@NCDs-180 at −0.9 V, indicating enhanced electron transportation. Fig. 9c illustrates the variation in FE of N-MoS2@NCDs with different reaction temperatures (180 °C, 200 °C, 220 °C) [159]. The superior efficiency and low onset overpotential observed in N-MoS2@NCDs-180 can be attributed to the substantial nitrogen content in the sample, improved conductivity facilitated by NCDs, and alterations in the electronic structure of Mo atoms induced by the significant nitrogen doping [159].

The remarkable efficacy of Cdots is evident in the study conducted by S. Zhao et al. [177], where Cdots played a pivotal role in enhancing CO production in the ternary Au-CDots-C3N4 electrocatalyst by providing an excellent ability for CO2 and H+ adsorption onto the catalyst surface. Upon the introduction of Cdots in the Au-C3N4 composite, as depicted in Fig. 9 (d, e), the CO2 adsorption and H+ increased from ∼ 0.22 mmol/g and 0.18 g/g in Au-C3N4 to ∼ 0.34 mmol/g and 0.37 g/g for Au-CDots-C3N4, respectively. The study also revealed that Cdots were not the direct active sites for ECRR; instead, the synergistic effect of Au, Cdots, and C3N4 in the ternary composite promoted the electroreduction process through charge redistribution, enhanced electron mobility, and a lower energy barrier for CO2 reduction. The Au-CDots-C3N4 composite exhibited a higher Faradaic efficiency of CO production, specifically 79.8 % at −0.5 V, with a low overpotential of 190 mV compared to the 4 wt% Au-C3N4, which showed 63.6 % at −0.6 V. Fig. 9f illustrates the variation in Faradaic efficiencies of CO production catalyzed by C3N4, CDots, CDots-C3N4, 4 wt% Au-C3N4, 4wt% Au-CDots-C3N4, and Au nanoparticles (56.3 % at −0.7 V), demonstrating the influence of each component on the CO2 reduction reaction [177]. Additionally, the enhanced catalytic activity of CDots in CO production was demonstrated by J. Gao et al. [150], where carbon quantum dots (CQD) facilitated CO2 adsorption onto the catalyst surface due to their abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups and promoted CO desorption in ECRR. These CQDs underwent functionalization and were decorated onto the porous Ag surface using a precipitation method. Although CQDs were not the active sites for ECRR, their addition resulted in a significant increase in CO Faradaic efficiency from 43.5 % at −1.1 V to 83.2 % at −0.8 V, corresponding to a 1.9-fold and 6.2-fold enhancement in the CO current density (from 0.638 to 3.989 mA cm−2). This underscored the role of the CQD surface in the heightened selectivity and activity of ECRR towards CO, as the CQD-covered porous Ag composite exhibited the highest CO2 adsorption ability of ∼78 μmol g−1 at 1.2 atm [150].

In addition to fullerene and carbon dots in the category of 0D carbon-based materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have garnered significant attention due to their nonzero band gaps resulting from quantum confinement and edge effects. GQDs showcase unique electronic and optical properties, positioning them as suitable candidates for ECRR applications. Ranging in size from 2 to 20 nm, GQDs are derived by segmenting a graphene monolayer into smaller pieces comprising a few graphene monolayers, predominantly composed of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. In the context of ECRR, pristine GQDs exhibit low activity and selectivity, favoring the hydrogen evolution reaction [178]. When employing a pristine GQDs electrode, the hydrogen evolution reaction prevails, as GQDs can only initiate CO2 reduction at more negative potentials, with slower kinetics and very low efficiency. This primarily results in the production of CO, HCOOˉ, trace amounts of CH3COOˉ, and 5–6 % each of CH4 and C2H4 [179]. Thus, to enhance selectivity and activity in ECRR, it becomes imperative to fine-tune and optimize the electronic and physicochemical properties of GQDs. Various strategies, such as shaping, sizing, geometric alterations, functionalization, and heteroatom doping, can be employed to manipulate the properties of GQDs, thereby unlocking their tremendous potential in energy storage and fuel generation. Due to the enriched density of edge sites in GQDs, functionalization and heteroatom doping emerge as compatible options to augment activity and product selectivity in ECRR, given their capacity to modify the catalyst's electronic structure and immobilize reduction reaction intermediates [178,180–182].

T. Zhang et al. [183] undertook the functionalization of GQDs with both electron-donating (–OH and –NH2) and withdrawing (–COOH and –SO3) groups, aiming to investigate and modulate the electron distribution effects, i.e., microenvironment, on the product selectivity and activity for the ECRR to produce CH4. The study revealed that functionalizing GQDs with electron-donating groups favored the reduction of CO2 to CH4, achieving a Faradaic efficiency of 70.0 % at −0.95 V with a partial current density of −200 mA cm−2. Conversely, GQDs functionalized with electron-withdrawing groups exhibited low CO2 reduction, with a Faradaic efficiency of less than 20 %, and favored the hydrogen evolution reaction. This behavior is due to the maintenance of higher charge density by the electron-donating groups at the neighboring C or N active sites and electron interaction with the adsorbates and key intermediates. Furthermore, the paper investigated ECRR on three distinct GQDs types: pristine-GQDs (p-GQDs), reduced-GQDs (r-GQDs), and oxidized-GQDs (o-GQDs), each having different functional groups based on their synthesis methods. Transmission electron microscopy images of these GQDs are provided in Fig. 10 (a-d). Although all three forms contained a similar graphene structure with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups like –OH, –C=O, and –COOH, the variation in their percentage content predominantly affected the product selectivity and the rate of product generation. Among these, r-GQDs, containing a relative content of 84.5 % -OH functional groups, exhibited the highest CH4 production with a Faradaic Efficiency of 65 % at −0.98 V with a partial current density of −153 mA cm−2. Upon further reduction of the o-GQDs (ro-GQDs), the FE of CH4 increased to 70.0 % at −0.90 V, with a partial current density of −200 mA cm−2, and the observation of multi‑carbon products with a Faradaic Efficiency of 12.0 %. The FE of the three different types of GQDs are illustrated in Fig. 10e. Due to the electron-donating property of -OH functional groups, which differs from -C=O and -COOH, variations in the electron density in the neighboring active sites (carbon or nitrogen) occurred, leading to dissimilar catalytic activities. Consequently, a stronger electron-donating capability and a higher content of electron-donating groups resulted in higher Faradaic Efficiency and product selectivity. For example, with the increasing –NH2 atomic content from 3.9 at.% to 8 at.%, the Faradaic Efficiency of CH4 and the partial current density increased from 48 % and − 132 mA cm−2 to 70 % and − 201 mA cm−2 at −0.95 V [183].

To disrupt the charge neutrality inherent in carbon atoms within GQDs, nitrogen atoms are doped into these GQDs, introducing abundant electron density. This infusion enhances the ECRR, converting CO2 into various high-value hydrocarbons and oxygenates such as CH4, C2H4, and C2H5OH. Consequently, nitrogen-doped carbon-based materials emerge as a compelling alternative for CO2 reduction reactions compared to traditional Cu electrodes. X. Zou et al. [113] demonstrated that the incorporation of N atoms into the edges of GQDs increased active site density and strengthened the bonding of these active sites with *COOH intermediates, thereby promoting ECRR. Pyridinic N, formed at the edge sites during N-doping, facilitates CC bond formation more effectively than pyrrolic N or graphitic N, consequently enhancing the generation of multi‑carbon C2 and C3 products. Utilizing Nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs), their study revealed that, at lower overpotentials, HCOO− and CO were the primary products due to the facile 2eˉ transfer pathways. For CO formation in NGQDs, the *CHO intermediate was formed through the protonation of the adsorbed CO, as the energy of *COH formation is 1.9 eV higher than that of *CHO. This intermediate was subsequently converted into a fully saturated aldehyde intermediate structure through C − N bond formation, creating intermediate formaldehyde (CH2O). The pathway for CH4 formation in NGQDs followed these steps: CO2 → *COOH → *CO → *CHO (energy of *COH is 1.9 eV higher than that of *CHO) → *CH2O → *CH2OH (energy of *OCH3 is 1.56 eV higher than that of *CH2OH) → *CH2 (to produce CH3OH, *CH2OH would require a 1.7 eV energy barrier for C atom hydrogenation, and favorable protonation of O over C in *CH2OH) → *CH3 → CH4 [113].

J. Wu et al. [179] meticulously engineered nanometer-sized Nitrogen-Doped Graphene Quantum Dots (NGQDs), as depicted in the Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) image shown in Fig. 10f. They achieved this by exfoliating and cutting a graphene oxide precursor, thus introducing a high density of edge-site nitrogen-doping defects that facilitated the production of C2 products, specifically C2H4 and C2H5OH. At a low cathodic potential (− 0.26 V), only CO and HCOO− were observed. However, at potentials beyond −0.61 V, NGQDs favored CC bond formation, leading to the generation of various C2 and C3 products, in addition to CO and HCOO−, including CH4, C2H4, C2H5OH, CH3COO−, and n-C3H7OH (Fig. 11a). NGQDs demonstrated remarkable product selectivity, with a maximum FE of 31 % observed at −0.75 V for C2H4, 15 % at −0.86 V for CH4, and 16 % at 0.78 V for C2H5OH. The exceptional selectivity was attributed to the presence of high pyridinic N groups in the NGQDs, serving as active ECRR sites. Acidic CO2 molecules favored adsorption onto the Lewis basic pyridinic N group, as evidenced by the decrease in the percentage of pyridinic N from 65 % to 38 % and an increase in the percentage of pyrrolic N from 20 % to 50 % when measured post-experiment. This shift in binding energy of pyridinic N with CO2 adsorption contributed to the observed changes [169]. In addition to nitrogen doping, catalyst size and morphology played a significant role in ECRR. Comparing the edge defect sites of 1 nm NGQDs (with a density of exposed edge sites of 44 %) to Nitrogen-Doped Reduced Graphene Oxides (NRGOs), it was observed that NRGOs had nitrogen dopants mostly located in the basal planes (with a density of exposed edge sites of 0.05 %), resulting in slower reaction kinetics. Therefore, NRGOs exclusively generated CO in the ECRR at a considerably more negative potential of −0.90 V, contrasting with NGQDs (−0.61 V), as illustrated in Fig. 11b. At this higher negative potential, the NRGOs displayed the generation of hydrocarbon products, albeit with lower percentages compared to NGQDs—CH4 (6 %), C2H4 (5 %), and oxygenates C2H5OH (4 %). This disparity arose from the higher density of exposed edge sites in the NGQDs, generated through the pulverization of 1–3 μm diameter NRGO sheets into 1–3 nm NGQDs. This study underscores the dependence of ECRR activity and productivity on catalyst morphologies, in addition to doping-induced defects. In comparison to NGQDs and NRGOs, H2 generation dominated in the pristine GQDs electrode, requiring a more negative potential for activating CO2 reduction (Fig. 11c), consequently yielding low Faradaic efficiencies of less than 5–6 % for the hydrocarbons — CH4 and C2H4 [179].

Among numerous transition metals, copper (Cu) is renowned for its exceptional ECRR, leading to the production of multi‑carbon hydrocarbons and oxygenates such as CH4, C2H4, CH3COOH, and C2H5OH, exhibiting high efficiency beyond the 2eˉ transfer reduction reaction pathways. X. Lyu et al. [135] conducted a comprehensive investigation into the role and impact of the presence of trace amounts of Cu on NGQDs in the production and selectivity of multi‑carbon products. As the Cu loading onto NGQDs increased, the FE and partial current density of C2 products (C2H4, CH3COOˉ, and CH3CH2OH) rose, and the potential shifted positively in CO reduction reactions. The predominance of CH4 production was observed in CO2 reduction reactions until the Cu loading reached 14.1 μg/cm2. However, in CO reduction reactions, the FE of CH4 decreased from 62 % to <10 % at −0.85 V when the Cu loading exceeded 2.5 μg/cm2. At 2.5 μg/cm2 Cu loading, the FE of CH3COOˉ exhibited 44.4 % with a partial current density of 117 mA/cm2 at −0.67 V in the CO reduction reaction. Further increasing the Cu loading to 3.5 μg/cm2, FEs of C2 products approached 80 % in the potential range of −0.65 V to −0.75 V. Both the partial current density and FE of C2 products increased with the growing Cu content in NGQDs, owing to the increased exposure of Cu on NGQDs surfaces, facilitating protonation of CO* to CHO* or OCCO* due to the high partial pressure of CO and strong H* chemisorption. The addition of Cu on the catalyst altered the reaction pathways, enabling a switch from C1 to C2 products in CO reduction reactions. This product switching was more feasible in CO reduction reactions compared to CO₂ reduction reactions, primarily due to the Eley-Rideal mechanism, facilitating CC coupling, because of the high partial pressure of CO [135].

In addition to Cu, gold (Au) is recognized for its highly selective electrocatalytic activity in efficiently converting CO2 to CO at lower overpotentials, attributed to its low binding energy towards CO molecules. Consequently, J. Fu et al. [184] synthesized single-crystalline Au nanoparticles (SCAu NPs) decorated on NGQDs (NGQDs-SCAu NPs) using a hydrothermal method. The presence of Au on NGQDs surfaces enhanced electrochemical CO2 conversion to CO at an ultralow onset potential of −0.15 V vs RHE, reaching the highest efficiency of 93 % at −0.25 V (Fig. 11d). This synergistic effect between NGQDs and SCAu NPs enhanced the physisorption of COOH* intermediates over the 63 % pyridinic N sites of NGQDs and the edge sites of SCAu. Fig. 11e illustrates the free energy diagram for the reduction of CO2 to CO on the SCAu-plane, SCAu-edge, NC, and NC-SCAu at U = 0 V. Besides CO, H2 was also generated in the CO2 reduction process, and the variation of Faradaic Efficiencies for different samples under variable applied potential is shown in Fig. 11f [184].

Table 2 provides a comparative summary of the ECRR performance using various 0D carbon-based materials. The analysis highlights that a higher Faradaic efficiency for ECRR is generally achieved with neutral electrolytes such as KHCO₃, as opposed to alkaline electrolytes like KOH. Notably, CO emerges as the primary ECRR product in the KHCO₃ electrolyte, whereas CH₄ is predominantly formed in the KOH electrolyte, accompanied by higher current densities. This difference can be attributed to the alkaline environment near the electrode surface created by KOH, which provides a high concentration of OH− ions. These ions enhance the protonation of key ECRR intermediates, such as *CO, leading to CH₄ production while suppressing the competing HER. In contrast, the KHCO₃ electrolyte, operating as a bicarbonate buffer, neutralizes OH− ions via the reaction
HCO 3 −
+
OH −
↔
CO 3
2 −
+
H 2
O . This limits the availability of protons, favoring the formation of CO over CH₄. Additionally, bicarbonate serves as a reservoir for CO₂ (
CO 3
2 −
) through the equilibrium (
CO 3
2 −
+
H 2
O ↔
HCO 3 −

OH −
), ensuring a consistent CO₂ supply at the electrode surface. Furthermore, in concentrated KHCO₃ or at higher local pH, the suppressed equilibrium dynamics between bicarbonate and CO₂ sustain a higher interfacial CO₂ concentration. This mitigates CO₂ hydration kinetics, thereby enhancing CO production. Overall, the choice of electrolyte significantly influences the reaction pathways and efficiency of the CO₂ reduction reaction. Neutral electrolytes like KHCO₃ favor CO generation by moderating proton availability and CO₂ concentration, while alkaline electrolytes like KOH promote CH₄ formation by facilitating protonation processes. Thus, the choice of electrolyte plays a pivotal role in determining the reaction pathways and overall performance of ECRR. Y. Meng et al. [185] comprehensively summarized the advantages and limitations of various electrolytes, including aqueous (acidic, alkaline, and neutral) and non-aqueous (organic solvents, ionic liquids, and deep eutectic solvents) systems, highlighting their critical role in facilitating product dissolution and providing ionic conductivity. Acidic electrolytes such as H₃PO₄ and H₂SO₄ offer high carbon efficiency but are hindered by competing HER activity due to the high concentration of H+ ions. Alkaline electrolytes like NaOH and KOH, while providing low cathode overpotential, suffer from reduced carbon efficiency as OH− ions react with CO₂ to form bicarbonate precipitates, thereby compromising stability. Neutral electrolytes such as NaHCO₃ and KHCO₃ strike a balance, offering sustainable operation with high Faradaic efficiency; however, they exhibit high solution resistance, which can limit their effectiveness. Non-aqueous systems bring unique advantages. Organic solvents provide a wide potential window and high CO₂ solubility, while ionic liquids offer strong CO₂ adsorption capabilities, and deep eutectic solvents stand out for their cost-effective synthesis. However, these systems also face significant challenges, such as poor stability, high synthesis costs, and high viscosities, which can impede their practical applications. A comprehensive understanding of these trade-offs is essential for optimizing electrolyte selection to achieve efficient and stable ECRR performance [185].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic CO2 reduction in one-dimensional carbon-based materials
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs), one-dimensional carbon allotropes, have received significant attention for their effective role in acting as ion storage, electron transport media, and ensuring stability, leading to enhanced catalytic performance and selectivity. CNTs structure exhibit sp2 hybridized carbon‑carbon chemical bonds, resulting in a unique tubular cylindrical structure with diameters ranging from a few nanometers to micrometers. Various CNT structures, including single-walled CNTs and multi-walled CNTs, possess diverse electrical, chemical, mechanical, thermal, and optical properties based on their network structures [186]. While inherently inert and exhibiting poor electrocatalytic activity and selectivity, CNTs find extensive application as supports for other electroactive materials. They enhance electrical conductivity, stability, and provide a highly porous surface area for reactants and intermediates adsorption, thereby facilitating proton and electron transfer. To overcome the inert nature of CNTs, functionalization with organic molecules or treatment with acids to introduce functional groups is common. Additionally, incorporation with other active electrocatalyst materials is achieved through methods such as self-assembly deposition, chemical or plasma vapor deposition, impregnation, precipitation, electrodeposition, etc. [147].

Similar to zero-dimensional carbon-based materials, nitrogen-doped CNTs (N-CNTs) have gained attention, where catalytic activities predominantly originated from nitrogen defects. P. Sharma et al. [114] extensively studied the role of nitrogen defect functionalities and density on CNTs in the electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction. Various N-CNTs were synthesized employing different nitrogen-containing hydrocarbon precursors, namely dimethylformamide (DMF), acetonitrile (ACN), and triethylamine (TEA), as the C/N source. Ferrocene was utilized as the C/Fe precursor, and the synthesis was carried out using the liquid chemical vapor deposition technique at different growth temperatures (750 °C, 850 °C, and 950 °C). Among the nitrogen functionalities, including graphitic, pyridinic, and pyrrolic nitrogen in N-CNTs, a positive correlation with the catalytic effect, i.e., Faradaic efficiency, and a reduction in the onset potential were observed for graphitic and pyridinic nitrogen. Specifically, an increase in pyridinic and graphitic N content from 0.3 to 1.1 at.% and 0.8 to 3.5 at.%, respectively, resulted in an increased Faradaic efficiency from 14 % to 80 % in the NCNTs-ACN-850 sample (NCNTs prepared from ACN source at 850 °C), with the onset potential decreasing from −1.05 V to −0.7 V. This effect was attributed to the facilitation of proton-electron pair transfers in the N defects of the graphitic carbon network, the lowest absolute overpotential of 0.20 V for COOH* intermediate formation at the pyridinic N site due to the retention of a pair of electrons, and localized excess negative charge in the graphite basal plane. However, compared to pyridinic N, graphitic N had a lesser effect on catalytic activity, as electrons in the graphitic N are less accessible to CO2 due to their location in the π* antibonding orbital. Conversely, an opposite trend was observed for pyrrolic N content, where an increase in its content from 0.78 to 1.3 at.% resulted in decreased Faradaic efficiency from 40 % to 25 % and an increase in cathodic onset potential from −0.78 to −1.05 V. This phenomenon was attributed to the challenging accessibility of electrons for CO2 binding, as the geometry of this defect forces the lone pair of the nitrogen atom closer towards the center [114].

J. Wu et al. [187] conducted a study emphasizing the influence of nitrogen defects on product selectivity and electrocatalytic activity by employing a liquid chemical vapor deposition method to synthesize NCNTs. The Faradaic efficiency of NCNTs exhibited a significant increase, reaching 80 % at −0.78 V, in stark contrast to pristine CNTs, which demonstrated only 3.5 % efficiency at −1.38 V. This remarkable enhancement in catalytic activity in NCNTs can be primarily attributed to the presence of N-doping, particularly pyridinic N acting as the active catalytic sites and providing high electrical conductivity, low free energy for CO2 activation, suppressed hydrogen evolution reaction, and weak binding of CO molecules on NCNTs. However, it is noteworthy that this study presented contrasting results concerning the sequence of efficient N-configurations (pyridinic N > pyrrolic N > graphitic N) compared to the findings reported by P. Sharma et al. [114] (pyridinic N > graphitic N > pyrrolic N). In the case of NCNTs, the activation of CO2 required an onset potential of −0.3 V to form adsorbed COOH*, significantly lower than that of CNTs (−1.9 V). The formation of adsorbed *COOH intermediate is an uphill process by the first electron-proton pair transfer followed by a thermodynamically downhill pathway for the formation of CO, involving a second electron-proton pair transfer process [187]. These results highlight the intricate interplay of nitrogen configurations in shaping the electrocatalytic performance of carbon nanotubes in CO2 reduction reactions.

In addition to nitrogen-doped CNTs, Y. Lum et al. [188] investigated the influence of metal impurities on the product selectivity and activities of these carbon-based electrocatalysts. The investigation involved carbon-based materials, including commercially available and nitric acid-washed variants with varying purity levels such as pure graphite (PG which has the lowest impurities with 0.01 ppm of Cu and ∼4 ppm of Fe), graphene oxide (GO contains the highest level of impurities with ∼3800 ppm Mn, ∼120 ppm Cu, ∼53 ppm Fe, ∼4 ppm Ni), and carbon nanotubes (CNTs that contains lower impurities than GO ∼1200 ppm Ni, ∼90 ppm Fe, ∼11 ppm Mn, ∼3 ppm Cu). These materials were then dispersed onto a glassy carbon (GC) electrode substrate. Observations revealed that metal impurities, particularly Cu, Ni, Fe, and Mn present in the as-procured carbon-based materials, played a significant role in product outcomes. Cu was identified as a key contributor to CH4 production (∼4.3 %) in GO, while Ni impurities predominantly contributed to H2 generation (∼100 %) in CNT. The study suggested a correlation between current densities of these carbon-based materials and the level of metal impurities. Treatment of GO/GC with high-purity nitric acid eliminated CH4 production, leading to a decrease in current density from 3.2 mA cm−2 to >1 mA cm−2. Post-treatment of PG/GC, CNT/GC, and GO/GC electrodes resulted in predominantly H2 generation, with traces of HCOOH (0.8–2 %) and CO (5–13 %). These traces were attributed to the intrinsic electrocatalytic properties of the carbon-based materials and induced defects from the acid treatment.

Introducing Cu nanoparticles via electrodeposition onto the defects of these carbon-based materials led to variations in ECRR activity and CH4 product selectivity. For instance, increasing Cu concentration from 0.01 ppm to 2 ppm in the GO/GC electrode resulted in an increase in current density from 0.005 mA cm−2 to 2.005 mA cm−2 and varied CH4 activity from 3.50 to 6.17 mmol/g s−1 [188]. The impact of Cu on CNTs for CH4 product selectivity was also explored by E. Akinoglu et al. [131]. They prepared a nanoid forest of vertically aligned freestanding multi-walled carbon nanotube arrays coated with Cu nanoparticles (MWCNT@Cu) through conformal coating. This unique structure exhibited a dramatic increase in ECRR CH4 product selectivity and activity compared to pristine MWCNTs. The encapsulation of MWCNTs with Cu nanoparticles reduced the onset potential from −0.67 V to −0.42 V, favoring increased CO diffusion, re-adsorption, and efficient transfer of coupled electrons and protons. The localized vertical voids within the MWCNT@Cu pillars effectively confine reactants, intermediates, and protons, facilitating the simultaneous transfer of coupled electrons and protons, while the curved region at the pillar head induces a high local electric field for a controlled microenvironment and pH. Consequently, the vertically aligned MWCNT@Cu pillars perform multiple crucial roles: influencing pH distribution, reactants, intermediates, and proton adsorption, enhancing electron conductivity by the inner CNT core, and shaping the local electric field to predominantly generate CH4 from the head region of the pillars [131].

Besides copper, gold (Au) is also a promising material for its high selectivity in the ECRR for CO generation. T. Huan et al. [148] synthesized a layer-by-layer assembly of Au nanoparticles on carbon nanotubes (AuCNTs), employing an amphiphilic nitrilotriacetic-diyne lipid (DANTA) as the first layer on the CNTs, cationic poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDADMAC) as the second layer, and 3 nm-sized Au nanoparticles as the third layer, as illustrated in Fig. 12a. In this configuration, Au served as the active electrocatalytic material, while CNTs acted as the porous scaffold, providing a higher surface area for enhanced product selectivity and Faradaic efficiency. In the absence of Au nanoparticles, no intrinsic catalytic activity was observed; instead, it functioned as an active material for H2 production. However, with an Au loading of 90 μg cm−2 on the CNTs, a CO Faradaic yield of 70 ± 2 % was observed at −0.55 V vs RHE, along with a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and additional H2 production of 25 ± 2 %. Although Au nanoparticles exhibited a higher yield of 75 ± 2 % for CO production at −0.55 V, AuCNTs demonstrated a much higher current density compared to Au nanoparticles (2 mA cm−2), indicating superior catalytic activity attributed to the larger surface area of porous CNTs [148]. In addition to utilizing DANTA and PDADMAC organic molecules for anchoring metal nanoparticles, Z. Ma et al. [146] employed a small amount of pyridine groups loaded onto CNTs as stability promoters, demonstrating a favorable synergy between Au and pyridine groups in enhancing catalytic activity, stability, and product selectivity. These pyridine groups were chemically anchored onto the CNTs surface through a diazo-reaction in an axial manner to form hydrogen bonds with *COOH intermediates and the nitrogen atom of the pyridine group, as depicted in Fig. 12b. The presence of these pyridine group promoters ensured the uniform distribution of ∼4 nm Au nanoparticles on the CNTs, enhancing conductivity. The pyridine-Au coating on oxidized CNTs (Au/Py-CNTs-O) exhibited a ∼ 93 % CO Faradaic efficiency across a wide potential range of −0.58 V to −0.98 V, surpassing that of Au/CNTs-O (89 % at −0.78 V). The main steps of the optimized structures for CO formation on the Au/Py-CNTs-O electrode surface are illustrated in Fig. 12c, while Fig. 12d presents the free energy diagram for CO production on Au/CNT and Au/Py/CNT electrode surfaces. In the absence of pyridine groups or Au, both CNTs-O and Py-CNTs-O exhibited poor ECRR performance. This inefficiency was attributed to the lack of electroactive sites in CNTs-O and the inaccessibility of the axial pyridine groups in Py-CNTs-O for chemical interaction with CO₂ molecules.
