This underscores the crucial synergy between axial pyridine and Au, stabilizing *COOH intermediates through hydrogen bond formation, ultimately reducing activation energy and overpotential by enhancing current density and concentrating CO2 molecules on the support surfaces [146]. Furthermore, these polymer coatings on CNTs provided stabilization to the electrocatalyst's active precious metals, inhibiting catalyst degradation or aggregation and facilitating stable electrocatalytic performance [147].

Delving deeper into metal-doped carbon-based materials for the ECRR, catalysts incorporating atomically dispersed metals (Ni, Fe, Co, etc.) and nitrogen co-doping (M-N/C) have attracted considerable attention. This attention stems from their ability to exhibit high product selectivity and activity, achieved by enhancing electron transfer and CO2 adsorption through the formation of suitable M-N-C bonds (Fig. 13a). In the study by Y. Kim et al. [143], the 1.37 Ni-N/NCNT (1.37 wt% Ni) and 3.02 Ni-N/NCNT (3.02 wt% Ni) electrocatalysts demonstrated maximum CO production Faradaic efficiencies of 97.83 % (at - 0.56 V) and 97.71 % (at - 0.41 V), respectively (Fig. 13b). The structural representation of the Ni-N/NCNT catalysts is depicted in Fig. 13a. This superior performance was attributed to the increased number of active sites or Ni sites, enhancing CO2 adsorption capacity, and consequently benefiting CO2 activation and reduction, resulting in higher FE and partial current densities. The electronic structure of the central active site, specific to the type of metal, played a crucial role, with Ni-N-C exhibiting the best ECRR performance due to its weak *H binding, leading to higher selectivity for CO as the ECRR product. In the absence of Ni active sites and N doping, NCNT and CNT exhibited low CO FE of <2.6 % and < 0.4 %, respectively. Moreover, the atomically dispersed Ni on N-doped CNTs (NiSA-N-CNTs) exhibited relatively stable performance, displaying FE of 89.0 % (at - 0.55 V) and 91.3 % (at - 0.7 V) for CO production. In NiSA-N-CNTs, NiN species served as the active centers for CO2 reduction to CO. The variation and increase in the partial current density of the 1.37 Ni-N/NCNT (1.37 wt% Ni) and 3.02 Ni-N/NCNT (3.02 wt% Ni) are depicted in Fig. 13c; with the increase in Ni content, the current density increased [143]. The CO yield of NiSA-N-CNTs, as reported by Y. Cheng et al. [189], was found to be 4 × 10−3 mmol cm−2 min−1, which was 6 to 7 times higher than the CO yield produced by N-CNTs and Ni-N-CNTs and twice as high as Ni-CNTs. In terms of partial current density, NiSA-N-CNTs exhibited 23.5 mA cm−2 at −0.7 V, which was four times that of Ni-N-CNTs, nine times that of N-CNTs, and 95 times that of Ni-CNTs. However, compared to the use of atomically dispersed Ni atoms on N-CNTs, the replacement of the Ni active center by Co, CoNi, and CoFe resulted in lower partial current densities yielding 2.7 mA cm−2, 0.8 mA cm−2, and 1.1 mA cm−2 at −0.4 V, respectively [189].

In metal-doped carbon-based electrocatalyst materials, the metal particles serve as the primary active centers for the ECRR. However, the continuous reaction process can lead to catalyst degradation and corrosion, thereby diminishing the catalyst's lifetime and efficiency. To address this issue, Y. Niu et al. [144] synthesized a Ni-based dual chainmail catalyst, Ni@NC@NCNT, which offers dual protection to the metal core. This design aimed to enhance stability, electrochemical performance, maintained the local alkaline environment, and suppressed H2 evolution and metal aggregation. SEM images of Ni@NC/NCNT and Ni@NC are displayed in Fig. 13 (d, e), while the TEM image of Ni@NC@NCNT is presented in Fig. 13f. In this configuration, the Ni core nanoparticles were enveloped by N-doped carbon layers (NCs) and nanotubes (NCNTs), providing protection and enabling directional charge transfer towards the carbon shells. Consequently, this modulated the local electronic properties of the carbon and reduced the work function. The dual chainmail Ni@NC@NCNT exhibited significantly higher CO production efficiency (94.1 % at −0.75 V) compared to bare NCNT (39.3 %), single chainmail Ni@NC electrocatalyst (18.4 %), and Ni@NC sitting on the NCNT walls (Ni@NC/NCNT) (29.3 %), as illustrated in Fig. 13g. The superior performance of Ni@NC@NCNT among the three Ni-containing samples can be attributed to its unique structural and compositional features. The high content of Niδ+ ions in Ni@NC@NCNT facilitated a robust interaction between the Ni core and the N-doped carbon shell. This interaction enhanced charge transfer from the Ni core to the carbon shell, a key factor enabled by the distinctive chainmail-like nanostructures. Furthermore, Ni@NC@NCNT demonstrated an exceptional BET surface area of 240.4 m2 g−1, significantly higher than the other samples, with mesoporous structures that provided abundant active sites and improved mass transport pathways for the ECRR. The double chainmail structure also contributed to the highest double-layer capacitance (Cdl) value of 13.5 mF cm−2, underscoring the material's ability to enhance ECRR kinetics. Additionally, Ni@NC@NCNT exhibited the highest pyridinic nitrogen content among the three Ni-containing samples, with pyridinic N identified as the primary active sites for ECRR. In comparison, although NCNT displayed a higher pyridinic N content than Ni@NC@NCNT, the absence of a Ni core in NCNT limited its performance. This was reflected in its lower Faradaic efficiency (39.3 %), reduced current density, smaller Cdl (6.8 mF cm−2), and lower BET surface area (167.6 m2 g−1). This enhanced performance is attributed to the synergistic effects of larger electrochemically active surface areas, abundant pyridinic N serving as the principal active sites, and Niδ+ catalytic active sites responsible for charge transfer from metallic Ni nanoparticles to the carbon shell through NiN and NiO bonds. This mechanism increased CO2 adsorption and enhanced the reduction kinetics to CO. Remarkably, the dual chainmail Ni@NC@NCNT exhibited no catalyst degradation even after continuous 43 h of electrolysis (> 90 % stability). Therefore, it demonstrated long-term stability, catalytic durability, and structural robustness, primarily attributed to the dual-layer protection provided by the CNTs and carbon layers [144].

To enhance catalytic performance and efficiency, Q. Zhu et al. [190] employed ionic liquids, which serve as co-catalysts for the ECRR and as supporting electrolytes influencing catalytic reactions. Ionic liquids proved to be advantageous in the ECRR due to their adjustable chemical and physical properties, high CO2 solubility, conductivity, and wide electrochemical window. The team deposited a composite of graphene oxide and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (GO/MWCNT) on carbon paper, a metal-free electrode. This composite demonstrated electrocatalytic selectivity for CO production with a FE of 85 % and a current density of 2.3 mA cm−2 at −2.2 V in the optimized [Bmim] BF4 (90 wt%)/MeCN binary mixture solution as the electrolyte [190].

Among metal-free, high-surface-area, and low-cost CO2 electrocatalysts, N-doped porous carbon materials are notable for their chemical stability, tunable conductivity, and electrochemical activity. However, the conventional use of Nafion or PVDF to bind N-doped porous carbon powders compromises catalytic performance and durability by deteriorating electrical conductivity, impeding contact between the electrolyte and the catalyst, and leading to catalyst detachment due to weak mechanical binding. Addressing these challenges, H. Wang et al. [110] designed hierarchically structured porous N-doped carbon membranes (HNCMs)/CNT as binder-free electrodes (HNCM/CNT) for ECRR conversion to formate. These membranes exhibited a range of pore sizes, each serving a distinct purpose. Macropores acted as mass transport highways, ensuring efficient movement, while micropores and mesopores contributed to a substantial surface area and provide accessible electroactive sites. The favorable benefits were further enhanced by the incorporation of nitrogen species into the carbon framework, providing electroactive sites and electrochemical stability. The HNCM/CNT electrode exhibited an 81 % FE for formate production in ECRR, attributed to the synergistic effects of high pyridinic nitrogen content, coupled edge terminations, and the preferred binding of CO2 to HNCM/CNT [110].

In the realm of one-dimensional carbon-based materials, graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) emerge as high-performance candidates for supporting electrocatalytic materials, owing to their structural and electronic tunability, mechanical flexibility, and high porosity. GNRs enhance the interaction between electroactive nanoparticles and the support, thereby possessing the ability to fine-tune catalytic stability and performance. By immobilizing metallic nanoparticles on the inert carbon-based GNR matrix, a Mott-Schottky heterojunction interface was formed, providing advantages for charge transfer and, consequently, regulating catalytic performance. Despite GNRs exhibiting electrochemically inert properties, C. Rogers et al. [149] synthesized Au nanoparticles embedded on the GNR matrix through a bottom-up synthesis process, demonstrating greater electrochemical surface area (ECSA) and a higher faradaic efficiency (> 90 %). The use of a GNR/Au composite matrix significantly boosted catalytic output by over 100 times compared to traditional amorphous carbon/Au nanoparticle composites, attributed to increased ECSA and improved mass transport within the matrix. The catalytic performance was notably influenced by the type of GNRs; for instance, cove-type GNRs, with lower work functions than traditional carbon black or graphene, promoted charge transfer and migration towards higher work function metal nanoparticles at the nanoparticle-semiconductor interface. This phenomenon increases electron density at the metal surface, thereby regulating the onset potential of the catalytic reaction [149].

Exploring the various configurations of graphene nanoribbons (GNRs), G. Zhu et al. [191] studied the influence of zig-zag and armchair edges terminated GNR with Cu atoms (ZGNR>Cu and AGNR>Cu) on the modulation of selectivity, electronic structures, and catalytic efficiency in the ECRR. Specifically, they denoted Cu-terminated zigzag GNRs as ZGNR>Cu and Cu-terminated armchair GNRs as AGNR>Cu. The introduction of Cu decoration on AGNRs resulted in a narrower band gap due to the addition of conduction band minima, transforming the bandgap from direct to indirect as compared to pristine AGNRs. This reduction in bandgap led to decreased overpotential and enhanced selectivity for CH3OH production during CO2 reduction. Additionally, the band gap of AGNR>Cu was found to be dependent on ribbon width, expressed as E
g (3p + 1) > E
g (3p) > E
g (3p + 2), where p = an integer, and its potential-limiting free energy followed the order ΔG (3p + 1) > ΔG (3p) > ΔG (3p + 2). In the AGNR>Cu electrocatalyst, the ribbon width with n = 3p + 2 demonstrated the most favorable ECRR, exhibiting a free energy barrier of <0.5 V. Conversely, the performance of ZGNR>Cu was found to be independent of ribbon width. Since Cu served as the electrocatalytic active sites for CO2 reduction, its proximity to neighboring Cu atoms influenced catalytic performance, impacting its exposure and ability to facilitate interactions with CO2 molecules. The study also highlighted product selectivity (methane and methanol) based on two different types of achiral GNR families due to distinct catalytic reaction pathways [191].

In addition to the aforementioned research, F. Pan et al. [82] demonstrated that the CO adsorption energy on both zigzag and armchair edges is approximately 0.3 eV weaker than that on the graphene nanoribbon (GNR formed by the unzipping of CNT through acid treatment, KMnO4 as oxidant) and CNT bulk sites. This weaker adsorption energy facilitated easier CO desorption from edge-located sites compared to basal plane sites. Depending on the type of anchoring substrate in the atomically dispersed Fe − N4 sites (anchored on CNT and GNR, denoted as Fe-N/CNT@GNR) or CNT or GNR, the host sites for Fe − N4 differed. The schematic illustration in Fig. 14a depicts the unzipping of commercial multi-walled CNTs doped with Fe to form the Fe-N/CNT@GNR structure. Fig. 14b illustrates the structural evolution from CNTs to CNT@GNR to GNR by adjusting KMnO4:CNT mass ratios. Fig. 14 (c1-g2) displays TEM images of the samples prepared with varying KMnO4:CNT mass ratios at different scales (100 nm and 10 nm). In Fe-N/CNT@GNR, Fe − N4 can bind to four different sites: CNT, GNR bulk plane, GNR zigzag edges, and GNR armchair edges. Conversely, in Fe-N/CNT, the CNT framework exclusively hosts the atomic Fe − N4 sites (Fig. 14a). In these atomically dispersed metal and nitrogen co-doped carbon (CNT or GNR or CNT and GNR) catalysts, Fe − N4 complexes served as the adsorption and binding sites for reaction intermediates. The local carbon structure significantly influenced the ECRR performance by determining the accessibility of active sites and mass transport. Notably, a high density of M − N x complexes anchored on the outermost walls enhanced accessibility for reactants and intermediates, favoring efficient CO2 reduction and suppressing H2 evolution compared to those confined in the inner walls of carbon nanotubes. The ECRR activity also depended upon the catalyst structures, with the order being Fe-N/CNT@GNR-1 (KMnO4:CNT = 1) < Fe-N/CNT@GNR-3 (KMnO4:CNT = 3) < Fe-N/CNT@GNR-2 (KMnO4:CNT = 2). Specifically, Fe-N/CNT@GNR-2, denoted as Fe-N/CNT@GNR, exhibited the highest CO production FE of 98 % at −0.76 V (28.3 mA cm−2), surpassing Fe-N/CNT (82 %) and Fe-N/GNR (92 %) with lower FE at −0.76 V. This observation underscores the synergistic effect of the integrated architecture of the GNR and CNT complex in Fe-N/CNT@GNR-2. The integrated structure provides four different Fe − N4 moieties (Fig. 15a), enhancing available Fe sites for the CO2 reduction reaction, where *COOH intermediates configure on different sites (Fig. 15b). However, introducing more Fe to the system (5 wt% Fe) resulted in reduced CO production efficiency, as Fe is known to support the hydrogen evolution reaction. This dependence of FE and partial current density for CO2 reduction to CO is highlighted across different Fe-N/C electrocatalysts, as shown in Fig. 15 (c,d) [82]. Consequently, optimizing the architecture of carbon-based supporting materials is essential for achieving efficient FE and stability.
Table 3 provides a comprehensive overview of the electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction performance achieved with various types of one-dimensional carbon-based materials serving as electrocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction in 2D carbon-based materials
ce:section.ce:para: Within the realm of two-dimensional carbon-based materials, graphene, a single carbon layer and its derivatives, are subjects of extensive study due to their distinctive physicochemical, optical, mechanical, and thermal properties. Graphene's honeycomb lattice structure, formed by sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, imparts significant attributes such as a large surface area (∼ 2630 m2 g−1), providing a high density of active surface sites, excellent thermal and charge conductivity, superior chemical stability, easy tunability, and acting as a stable anchoring substrate for various molecular structures and nanoparticles. Consequently, graphene finds applications in diverse fields such as sensing, environmental remediation, energy storage and conversion, catalysis, and more. In the context of the ECRR, graphene plays a pivotal role in promoting electron transfer and conductivity while serving as an anchor for electroactive materials, including metals, non-metals, or molecular substances. While pristine graphene exhibits inertness towards the electrochemical CO2 reduction process due to its neutral carbon atoms, its physicochemical and electrical properties can be tailored through various synthesis and post-treatment techniques. These techniques include annealing in different environments, acid treatment, chemical exfoliation, solvothermal processes, chemical vapor deposition, and more. Moreover, the inertness of pristine graphene to bind CO2 molecules and the challenge of overcoming the substantial free energy barrier in forming stable *COOH intermediates can be addressed by modifying its chemical reactivity and electronic properties. This can be achieved through strategies such as doping with heteroatoms (metals, non-metals, noble elements), introducing defects, incorporating oxygenated functional groups, functionalization, inducing defect creation, and integrating active molecular or organic polymer catalysts. These modifications enhance the ECRR performance by inducing asymmetric charges on the graphene surface, modifying its electronic structure, and providing a high density of active sites [180–182].

The influence of topological defects on the ECRR performance, chemical stability, and durability of graphene has been thoroughly investigated by P. Han et al. [194]. Various topological defects were induced in graphene using distinct annealing techniques and plasma treatments, forming different active catalytic sites for CO2 reduction reactions. The synthesized defects included:
(1)
N-doped graphene (NG): Formed by annealing pristine graphene under NH3.
(2)
Defective graphene (DG): Formed by annealing N-doped graphene at 1150 °C under an Ar atmosphere.
(3)
Edge-rich graphene (EG): Formed by plasma etching of graphene.

The synthesis process of defective graphene (DG) is illustrated in Fig. 16a, and its electrocatalytic reduction of CO2, along with TEM images, is depicted in Fig. 16b and c for DG and edge-rich graphene, respectively. Among these, DG exhibited the highest Faradaic efficiency of 84 % at −0.6 V and a current density of 2.1 mA cm−2 (Figs. 16d, e). This superior performance can be attributed to its extensive electrochemical surface area (ECSA), providing a high density of catalytically active sites and CO2 chemisorption capacity, along with exceptional electronic conductivity. Fig. 16f illustrates the variation in Faradaic efficiency for CO2 reduction to CO and H2 production at different applied potentials when DG was employed as the electrocatalyst. DG fabrication involved introducing defects and a nitrogen removal method from N-doped graphene, with the defects visibly concentrated at the edges of the graphene layer. In contrast, plasma treatment of pristine graphene produced in-plane nanoholes distributed across the graphene surface. This study demonstrated a significant increase in catalytic activity with an elevated defect concentration, highlighting the correlation with the density of catalytically active sites, chemisorption capacity, and electronic conductivity [194].

The selectivity of the ECRR product was significantly improved through the utilization of 5 nm Bi nanoparticles uniformly distributed on ultrathin reduced graphene oxide (Gr) nanosheets. This approach mitigated nanoparticle aggregation and enhanced electron transfer distribution in the metal-support interface (Bi/Gr) by modifying the electronic structure. The hydrothermally synthesized Bi/Gr hybrid catalysts (with a mass ratio of Bi(NO3)3/GO = 1), as reported by D. Wu et al. [195], demonstrated the highest Faradaic efficiency for formate production at 92.1 % at −0.97 V. This outperformed the physically mixed Bi-Gr electrocatalyst (83.2 %) and other mass ratios of Bi(NO3)3/GO, namely 0.1Bi/Gr, 0.5Bi/Gr, and 3Bi/Gr, considering that Bi served as the catalytically active site. The formation of Bi/Gr led to the facilitation of Bi3+ ions by the oxygen-containing functional groups of graphene oxide, reducing graphitic N and pyridinic N due to the incorporation of Bi heteroatoms in the Gr nanosheets. With the optimized mass ratio of Bi(NO3)3/GO, the Bi/Gr sample exhibited enhanced performance, selectivity, and durability. This improvement was attributed to the synergy resulting from the robust interaction between Bi and Gr, facilitating surface charge transfer, high CO2 adsorption capacity, and intermediate bindings, thereby increasing overall efficiency [195].

Numerous studies have investigated the ECRR conversion to C1, C2, or C2+ products using N-doped graphene electrocatalysts, capitalizing on the diverse catalytically active sites formed by nitrogen and carbon moieties for CO2 and intermediate docking. K. Chang et al. [196] conducted a thorough examination of twenty-six active sites across 14 graphene models, comprising nitrogen and carbon, by employing N-doped graphene as the model electrocatalyst. Their aim was to assess the capability of metal-free N-doped graphene in facilitating CO2 reduction reactions under constant applied potentials. From the 26 active sites considered (illustrated in Fig. 17 ), only 15 proved capable of forming stable abduct CO2 molecules under an applied potential of −1 V. However, under zero charge conditions, five (2-C, 6-N, 8-N, 9-N, and 11-C2) of these 15 sites were unable to abduct CO2 molecules. Among the catalytically active sites, 7-N and 8-N exhibited the ability to bind CO after its formation, requiring free energy for desorption values of 0.08 eV and 0.58 eV, respectively. Specifically, the CO bonded with two bonds for 7-N (one with pyrrolic and another with pyridinic) and three bonds for 8-N with pyridinic N, while other sites formed only one bond with either N or C. Consequently, the 7-N and 8-N sites demonstrated the capacity to produce CH4.

The free energy surfaces associated with the ECRR to CO or *CO on 11-C1, 12-C, 7-N, and 8-N are visually represented in Fig. 18 (a-d). Fig. 18e depicts the ECRR process on N-doped graphene, while Fig. 18f showcases the various types of N-dopants present in the N-doped graphene electrocatalyst. When considering the reduction of CO to CHO, it encounters substantial free energy barriers for CO2 adsorption values exceeding 0.97 eV. Consequently, further reactions beyond the two-proton–two-electron reduction were deemed unlikely. However, N-doped graphene had demonstrated its capability to produce CO or surface-bound CO through CO2 reduction. The free energies of *OCHO intermediates were found to be larger than those of *COOH by more than 0.6 eV, favoring the *CO2 → *COOH pathway over *CO2 → *OCHO in N-doped graphene. The sites responsible for CO production consisted of carbon atoms situated on the edges of graphene, along with adjacent graphitic nitrogen atoms possessing either pyridinic or pyrrolic N. Accordingly, the study suggested that an increase in N-doped concentration, encompassing both graphitic and pyridinic N, leads to a decrease in CO2 adsorption free energies. These CO2 adsorptions exhibited a positive correlation under more negative applied potential, as a more negative potential induced a negatively charged surface conducive to CO2 adsorption through electron transfer. Thus, the enrichment of edge carbon sites and an increase in graphitic N concentration are identified as strategies to enhance ECRR performance [196].

X. Sun et al. [197] conducted a comprehensive investigation into the reaction mechanism and ECRR activity across 11 distinct types of N-doped graphene sites, utilizing the density functional theory (DFT) method to elucidate the predominant catalytic active sites, including pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, graphitic N, and C adjacent to N, coexisting in carbon materials. In this study, armchair-edged graphene nanoribbons measuring 8.52 Å × 24.6 Å and zigzag-edged graphene nanoribbons measuring 25.6 Å × 9.84 Å were considered. The research revealed that the most favorable step involved the formation of HCOOH, followed by CO. Notably, variations in active site types resulted in different potential predominant steps, such as CO2 → *CO2 for Zigzag-pyridinic N (Z-pyN) and *CO2 → *COOH for Zigzag-Graphitic N (Z-GN). Upon analyzing the free energy barriers for the formation of HCOOH as a CO2 reduction product, Z-GN (free energy barrier = 0.39 eV) and Z-pyN-doped graphene (free energy barrier = 0.38 eV) exhibited superior catalytic activity compared to other configurations. In addition to HCOOH, Z-pyN-doped graphene also produced CH4 and CH3OH as minor products with free energy barriers of 0.83 eV and HCHO with a free energy barrier of 0.81 eV. Schematic illustrations depicting the free energy changes in the ECRR pathways on various N-doped graphene configurations are presented in Fig. 19 [197].

For improved product selectivity, the incorporation of metal nanoparticles proves instrumental, with performance optimization achievable through careful adjustments in shape, size, and nanoparticle type. In the context of CC hydrocarbons, Cu nanoparticles are frequently utilized, and their size and concentration variations significantly influence the selectivity of ECRR products. In a study by Q. Li et al. [198], Cu nanoparticles of different sizes (7 nm and 13 nm) and mass ratios were employed on pyridinic N-rich graphene, and the ensuing ECRR outcomes were investigated. Among three distinct N-precursors used for pyridinic N-rich graphene preparation (ethylenediamine, melamine, polyaniline), melamine usage resulted in the highest pyridinic-N/graphitic-N ratio. Utilizing pyridinic N-rich graphene (p-NG), a formate production efficiency of 65 % was achieved at −0.9 V. However, when p-NG was combined with Cu nanoparticles (7 nm) at a mass ratio of p-NG:Cu = 1:1 (p-NG-Cu), a formate FE of 62 % was attained, with 97 % selectivity at −0.8 V. Conversely, further increasing the negative applied voltage to - 0.9 V, C2H4 was formed with a 19 % Faradaic efficiency and 79 % hydrocarbon selectivity. Notably, the use of larger 13 nm Cu nanoparticles at a higher negative potential (− 1.1 V) resulted in only 13 % C2H4 FE, indicating reduced selectivity and activity. This decline was attributed to the larger nanoparticle size limiting the interaction of the Cu nanoparticles (13 nm) with the pyridinic N sites in p-NG-Cu (13 nm), thus reducing the synergy between Cu nanoparticles and pyridinic N sites. In contrast, p-NG-Cu (7 nm) exhibited a proper combination facilitating CO2 reduction, hydrogenation, and subsequent CC coupling for C2H4 formation. Additionally, an increased concentration of Cu nanoparticles (p-NG:Cu = 1:2) led to uneven distribution on the p-NG surface due to nanoparticle overloading. Conversely, higher p-NG concentration resulted in insufficient Cu nanoparticles to cover the p-N sites adequately. Therefore, optimization of size and concentration is crucial to achieving enhanced product selectivity and activity [198].

In the investigation led by J. Yuan et al. [133], Cu nanoparticles were strategically integrated onto graphene oxide sheets modified by pyridoxine (VB6), an N-containing biologically active compound, as depicted in Fig. 20a, resulting in GO-VB6-Cu (10 wt% Cu). This hybrid catalyst demonstrated a formate production efficiency of 56.3 % at an impressively low overpotential of 0.14 V during the electrochemical CO2 reduction to ethanol (Fig. 20c). The ECRR process utilizing GO-VB6-Cu for the conversion of CO2 to CO is illustrated in Fig. 20b, and experiments were conducted in a two-compartment cell. The inclusion of Cu nanoparticles significantly reduced the overpotential for C2H5OH formation, exhibiting enhanced efficiency compared to GO-Cu (10.3 % at −0.42 V and 1.170 mA/cm2) and GO-VB6 (36.4 % at −0.4 V and 2.218 mA/cm2). With the increase of weight ratios of Cu during material synthesis, the sizes of Cu nanoparticles enlarged from 2 to 3 nm at 5 wt% and 10 wt% to 25 to 200 nm at 40 wt%, accompanied by agglomeration. Double-layer capacitance analysis revealed that the 10 wt% Cu content, i.e., GO-VB6-Cu, exhibited the largest electrochemically active surface area. Consequently, this configuration demonstrated maximum Faradaic efficiency and current density at low overpotential, low electron transfer resistance, augmented CO2 adsorption, and accelerated CO2 reduction kinetics. In the GO-VB6-Cu electrocatalyst, pyridinic N and Cu acted as electrocatalytic active sites, with CO2 •− intermediates generated by adsorbed CO2. Subsequently, a series of PCET processes formed CO* intermediates, which underwent dimerization to yield OC − CO* and ultimately led to C2H5 production. The observed CC coupling formation was attributed to the presence of Cu nanoparticles, well-known for their capability in multi‑carbon alcohol production [133].

Furthermore, in the investigation conducted by S. Dongare et al. [112], N-doped graphene decorated with 20 wt% Cu nanoparticles exhibited C2H5OH as the primary product, boosting a selectivity of 25.72 % and a current density of 7.7 mA/cm2 at −1.0 V. The superior performance of NGN-Cu (20 wt%) electrocatalyst, compared to other NGN configurations decorated with varying Cu wt%, was attributed to optimized Cu loadings. These loadings led to higher ECSA, facilitated enhanced mass transfer during the ECRR, and promoted the CC coupling rate through the dimerization of COOH*/CO* intermediates. The Faradaic efficiencies corresponding to different Cu loadings on NGN are illustrated in Fig. 20 (d-g) for Cu contents of 10, 20, and 30 wt%. Notably, the use of 30 wt% Cu loadings resulted in a decrease in current density due to the formation of agglomerates and an increase in crystallite sizes, consequently reducing the surface area and passivating available active sites. The enhanced performance of NGN with 20 wt% Cu loading was attributed to the synergistic effect of pyridinic N and Cu content, which improved structural and electronic properties, facilitating the formation of stable CO2 •− adsorption intermediates. The Tafel plot of these electrocatalysts is presented in Fig. 20h, showing the comparison of the reaction rate of the Cu20/NGN, NGN, Cu10/NGN, and Cu30/NGN electrocatalysts. In N-doped graphene loaded with Cu nanoparticles, pyridinic N acted as the site for CO production by facilitating CO2 reduction, while Cu triggered the formation of CC coupling, leading to the production of multi‑carbon products [112].

Among the transition metals incorporated into N-doped graphene, single-atom catalysts demonstrated the highest Faradaic efficiency due to the formation of cyclam-like structures. W. Bi et al. [199] developed a method to immobilize Ni2+ on N-doped graphene, creating cyclam-like moieties that served as dominant reactive sites through ion adsorption and a solution chemistry route, as illustrated in Fig. 21a. This synthesis approach eliminated the need for high-temperature pyrolysis and acid treatment, which are energy-intensive, environmentally unfriendly, and hinder practical implementation. The cyclam-like macrocycles with Ni2+ ions in N-doped graphene exhibited notable CO production performance, achieving a Faradaic efficiency of 92 % at −0.68 V, while pristine N-doped graphene exhibited <60 % Faradaic efficiency for CO at all potentials (Fig. 21a). Fig. 21b illustrates the free energy diagrams for the ECRR conversion to CO on Ni2+@NG and NG. This enhanced performance can be attributed to the introduction of Ni2+ ions on the N-doped graphene nanosheets, which reduced interface charge transfer resistance, facilitating CO2 reduction kinetics. The potential rate-limiting step for the Ni2+@NG electrocatalyst was the activation of CO2 to form adsorbed *COOH intermediates, while N-doped graphene showed the highest free energy barrier for forming chemisorbed *CO by protonating *COOH [199].

C. Zhang et al. [139] explored a Fe-centered cyclam-like structure in N-doped graphene, as illustrated in Fig. 22b. In this electrocatalyst, the synergistic effect of the monatomic dispersion of Fe on N-doped graphene, with a Fe–N4-based configuration serving as electrocatalytic active sites for CO2 reduction, was observed. The Fe/NG-750 °C (few Fe atoms of sub-nanometer-sized nanoclusters, 3–4 Å) exhibited a CO production Faradaic efficiency of 80 % at −0.57 V with a low overpotential. However, at higher potentials, Faradaic efficiency and current densities decreased as H2 evolution began to dominate on the electrode surface. Annealing of these samples also affected ECRR performance; for example, Fe/NG-800 (annealed at 800 °C) only exhibited 12 % CO production FE at −0.57 V, as shown in Fig. 22c, and showed aggregated metallic Fe nanoparticles (20 nm). The highest N-content was obtained at the annealing temperature of 700–750 °C. In the FeN4 moieties with optimized geometries, Fe acted as the catalytic site for PCET, resulting in the formation of Fe–N4–COOH intermediate, followed by proton-electron transfer to form *CO abduct by reduction. The final CO was obtained by the dissociation of the *CO abduct bond. Fig. 22d shows the free energy diagram of the ECRR to CO on different FeN4 moieties embedded on graphene sheets. In the FeN4 moieties in graphene, the energy barrier was 0.63 eV for *COOH formation, while in the FeN4 moieties with two graphitic N, the energy barrier decreased to 0.29 eV. Thus, nitrogen doping and the formation of FeN4 moieties on the graphene surface produced highly efficient CO2 reduction activity [139].

In addition to the aforementioned study, H. Zhang et al. [200] synthesized a Fe-based single-atom catalyst with graphene support using thermal pyrolysis, forming both FeN4 and FeN5 moieties in the Fe-N-C electrocatalyst. The addition of an excess amount of Fe to the defective graphene led to the formation of FeN5, anchoring on defective graphene through the creation of an additional axial ligand coordinated with FeN4 and the N-dopant. In comparison to FeN4 (67 %), FeN5 exhibited a maximum CO production faradaic efficiency of 97 % at −0.46 V, with a low overpotential of 0.35 V and current densities. This superior performance was attributed to the presence of an additional axial ligand in FeN5 compared to FeN4, despite having higher N and Fe content in FeN4. The high surface area provided by the graphene support and the enhanced conductivity resulting from the unique electronic structure of FeN5, formed through the interaction between pyrrolic N-doped graphene and Fe, contributed to enhancing the ECRR process and overall performance. To achieve high CO production efficiency, the free energy change and binding strength of *CO played a pivotal role. FeN5 moieties demonstrated a weak binding energy of *CO (0.54 eV), facilitating rapid CO desorption compared to FeN4 (CO desorption energy of 1.35 eV), where CO* strongly binds to the FeN4 catalytic active sites. In the FeN5 catalytic active sites, the electron density of Fe 3d orbitals was depleted due to the additional axial pyrrolic nitrogen ligand, thereby reducing the Fe-CO π-back donation from the metal d orbitals to the antibonding 2π* orbital of CO, resulting in high selectivity for CO production and fast CO desorption [200]. Therefore, N-doped graphene materials emerge as optimal supports for electroactive materials such as metal nanoparticles, polymers, heteroatoms, oxides, etc., providing a large electroactive surface area for CO2 adsorption, good conductivity for electrons and protons, and abundant functional groups.

The structural configuration or chirality of defective graphene, whether in a zigzag or armchair configuration, also significantly influences the ECRR performance due to variations in electron distribution, thus impacting the binding energies of formed intermediates. Armchair edge carbons, specifically, provide an optimal distance between metal atoms bonded to them, facilitating interactions with CO2 molecules via the exposed sites. The short metal‑carbon bond in the armchair configuration further promotes a higher number of electron transfers. Moreover, in N-doped graphene, pyridinic N serves as the proton source, CO2 absorber, and activation site, facilitating hydrogenation and CC coupling reactions on the active metal atoms.

Apart from 2D graphene, other 2D carbon nanosheets have also been employed as electrocatalysts due to their favorable structural properties, including high porosity, accessible active surfaces, and efficient mass transport. X. Liang et al. [201] synthesized metal-free N and P-doped porous carbon nanosheets (NPC) for electrocatalytic CO2 reduction to CO using a pyrolysis-controlled sacrificial templating approach. Citric acid served as the carbon source, phytic acid as the phosphorus source, and melamine along with NH3 as nitrogen sources. During pyrolysis, unstable P species were removed, generating pores in the carbon plane, while phytic acid aggregation acted as a sacrificial template. The resulting pores inhibited the formation of graphitic N, favoring the loading of pyridinic N, which enhanced mass transfer during the ECRR process. Among the synthesized catalysts (NC-900 without P doping, and NPC-900-1, NPC-900-2, and NPC-900-3 prepared using 148, 295, and 590 μL phytic acid solution, respectively), NPC-900-2 exhibited the highest micropore-specific surface area. This was attributed to the formation of new macropores or the extension and fusion of existing pores following the detachment of P species. NPC-900-2 demonstrated a remarkable Faradaic efficiency of 88 % and an optimal CO partial current density of 1.71 mA cm−2 at −0.41 V. This exceptional performance was ascribed to its high surface area, the synergistic effects of P and N within the porous carbon framework, the elevated pyridinic N content acting as active sites for ECRR, and the enhanced catalytic activity driven by increased porosity from P doping [201]. In another study, B. Zhang et al. [202] synthesized selenium-doped carbon nanosheets (Se-CNs) with hierarchical micro-, meso-, and macroporous structures using selenium dioxide, chitosan, and ammonium chloride as raw materials via pyrolysis. The Se-CNs, featuring selenium-optimized active sites, demonstrated superior CO Faradaic efficiency and selectivity, achieving 90 % at −0.6 V (vs. RHE), which was 11 times greater than undoped carbon nanosheets (CNs). This enhancement was attributed to the hierarchical porous architecture, which facilitated ion and reactant penetration, efficient transport, and reaction kinetics, while maximizing the accessible active surface area. The study further revealed that increasing KHCO3 concentration from 0.1 M to 0.5 M caused a decline in CO selectivity and Faradaic efficiency from 90 % to 78 % at −0.6 V (vs. RHE), likely due to competitive HER activity. The superior activity of Se-CNs was attributed to their stronger CO2 adsorption compared to hydrogen adsorption, whereas undoped CNs exhibited stronger hydrogen adsorption energies. Additionally, Se-CNs had a significantly lower free energy barrier for CO2 reduction (0.32 eV) compared to CNs (0.63 eV) and a higher energy barrier for *H2 formation (0.76 eV), enhancing their ECRR activity and selectivity. The study also highlighted the impact of chalcogen electronegativity on Faradaic efficiency and selectivity. Under similar experimental conditions, the CO Faradaic efficiency followed the order of Se-CNs (90 %) > (Se, Te)-CNs (88 %) > Te-CNs (86 %) at −0.6 V (vs. RHE). Selenium, being more electronegative than tellurium, induced a shift in the partial density of states closer to the Fermi level of CNs, effectively suppressing HER activity and enhancing CO2 reduction [202]. Therefore, defects in carbon-based materials, whether intrinsic or extrinsic, possess the ability to engineer the electronic and chemical properties of the electrocatalyst, providing varying concentrations of electrocatalytic active sites, thus affecting ECRR performance. Table 4 summarizes the ECRR performance using different types of 2D carbon-based materials as electrocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction in 3D carbon-based materials
ce:section.ce:para: In the family of three-dimensional carbon allotropes, diamond, characterized by sp3 hybridized carbon atoms arranged in tetrahedral bonding across the lattice, is emerging as a significant candidate for electrocatalysis in various applications. The distinctive carbon atom bonding in diamond, as opposed to other carbon allotropes, and its unique electronic properties result in varied responses to ECRR. The diamond's structure imparts exceptional attributes such as high thermal conductivity, hardness, and electrical resistivity. While pristine diamond behaves as an insulator, introducing dopants or creating defects, including sp2 hybridization, allows for the modification and tuning of the electrical properties of impure diamond. Nitrogen (n-type dopant) and boron (p-type dopant) are the primary dopants for diamond, with boron being preferred due to its smaller size, ease of displacing carbon atoms with a lower activation energy of 0.37 eV, and higher doping levels compared to nitrogen. Thus, in contrast to other carbon allotropes that exhibit superior ECRR activity with nitrogen doping, researchers favor boron doping in diamond to modulate electronic properties, influencing electrocatalyst performance in ECRR applications. Boron-doped diamond (BDD) stands out as a promising electrode in ECRR, offering a wide potential window for operation, optical transparency, reduced fouling, microstructural stability, and chemical stability. Utilizing BDD electrodes in CO2 electrocatalytic reduction yields various C1 products, including CO, HCOOH, CH3OH, and HCHO, contingent upon the electrolyte type and boron doping concentration [203,204].

In a study conducted by K. Natsui et al. [205], a BDD electrode was employed for the efficient production of formic acid (HCOOH) with an impressive Faradaic efficiency of 94.7 % in a two-compartment flow cell. This performance proved comparable to other metal electrodes such as Pb (97.4 %) and Sn (88.4 %). The investigation examined the impact of flow rate variations and current density on the electrocatalyst's capability for CO2 reduction to HCOOH. The Faradaic efficiency exhibited a positive correlation with the electrolyte flow rate in the flow cell, which is attributed to the increased mass transport of CO2 onto the BDD surface. On the contrary, the production rate of HCOOH displayed a negative correlation with current density. Specifically, the Faradaic efficiency declined from 94.7 % to 19 % for HCOOH production as the current density increased from 2 mA cm−2 to 20 mA cm−2. In contrast, an increase in the flow rate from 20 mL min−1 to 200 mL min−1 resulted in a Faradaic efficiency surge from 35.4 % to 94.7 %. The BDD electrode demonstrated an exceptional selectivity of 99 % for HCOOH production, primarily due to its limited ability to bind the HCOOH/HCOO− intermediates, stemming from the inert nature of the BDD electrode surface. Additionally, the stabilization of the CO2 •ˉ intermediate on the BDD surface was facilitated by electronic interactions arising from differences in electronegativities, such as the polarized BC bond and defects. Accordingly, the BDD electrode exhibited a pronounced inclination towards producing HCOOH as the primary product [205].

In another study done by K. Natsui's group [206], the investigation focused on the product selectivity and efficiency of BDD electrodes, considering two distinct electrolytes (KCl and KClO4) and varying boron doping concentrations. When KClO4 served as the catholyte, the primary product on the BDD electrode (1 % boron) was CO, while HCOOH dominated on the BDD electrode with 0.1 % boron when KCl was used as the catholyte (Fig. 23a, b). The proposed reaction pathway for the electrochemical CO2 reduction on the BDD electrode in KClO4 and KCl aqueous solutions is depicted in Fig. 23c. The observed disparity in product selectivity was attributed to the distinct anions in the catholyte. KClO4 was found to promote the adsorption of CO2 •ˉ intermediates on the electrode surface, as confirmed by in-situ attenuated total reflectance-infrared (ATR-IR) measurements. On the contrary, KCl hindered adsorption, leading to the predominant formation of HCOOH in the KCl catholyte. As boron serves as the active catalytic site for CO2 reduction, a boron content of 1 % created more holes and acceptor states for intermediate adsorption than the 0.1 % boron content, resulting in higher product selectivity. However, optimization of the dopant concentration is crucial; a further increase to 5 % boron content led to decreased faradaic efficiency compared to the 1 % boron content performance. Additionally, product selectivity and Faradaic efficiency were influenced by the applied potential, with the maximum selectivity for CO production and Faradaic efficiency achieved at −2.2 V. The study also explored the impact of flow rate on efficiency, revealing that higher flow rates enhanced mass distribution and facilitated CO removal from the electrode surface, allowing new CO2 to access the electrode surface. Notably, the study affirmed that BDD electrodes exhibited switchable product selectivity, a feature not observed on other metal electrodes such as Sn [206].

To further explore the dependency of CO2 reduction performance on the microenvironment of the electrode, K. Nakata et al. [207] conducted a comprehensive study on BDD electrocatalysis for CO2 reduction in various electrolytes: methanol, seawater, and water + NaCl, utilizing the experimental setup illustrated in Fig. 23d. In the case of methanol, where CO2 readily dissolves compared to water, the BDD electrode exhibited the production of HCHO with a remarkable faradaic efficiency of 74 % at −1.7 V (Fig. 23e). Seawater, chosen for its conductivity and abundance as a source of protons and electrons, resulted in a Faradaic efficiency of 36 % for HCHO production. Conversely, the aqueous NaCl solution (0.1 M) demonstrated an improved FE of 62 %. The lower Faradaic efficiency observed in seawater was attributed to impurities present as compared to the relatively pure NaCl aqueous solution. The generation of HCHO on the BDD electrode is noteworthy, primarily influenced by the presence of sp3-bonded carbon atoms. In contrast, materials like glassy carbon, rich in sp2-bonded carbon, can only produce 19 % HCHO through the two-electron reduction of formic acid [207]. This study highlights the significance of the electrolyte composition in tailoring the BDD electrocatalytic performance for CO2 reduction under different microenvironments.

While the predominant product on a BDD electrode is HCOOH with CO as a minor byproduct, the presence of sp2 carbon in the BDD electrocatalyst can significantly influence the Faradaic efficiency and selectivity of the ECRR (Fig. 23f). The study conducted by J. Xu et al. [208] underscores the substantial influence of sp2 hybridized carbon species on the ECRR performance and selectivity. The introduction of sp2 species enhances the production of CO and H2 as compared to HCOOH, given that sp2 surfaces possess a higher adsorption capacity for CO2 •ˉ intermediates, thereby impacting the overall selectivity of the ECRR. The incorporation of sp2 carbon altered the electronic structure and density of states, influencing the electrochemical properties of the BDD electrode. In the absence of sp2 species, the BDD electrode exhibited an 84 % Faradaic efficiency for HCOOH production, which significantly decreased to 18 % on a glassy carbon surface with an entirely sp2 species-laden surface. Moreover, BDD with a high sp2 content yielded 18 % HCOOH. Conversely, concerning H2 generation, it increased with the rising sp2 content on the electrode surface—from 11 % on a no-sp2 BDD to 71 % on a glassy carbon electrode. Beyond selectivity, the content and concentration of sp2 species also influenced the reduction potential, with no-sp2 BDD exhibiting a − 2.5 V reduction potential compared to a − 3.4 V reduction potential for high-sp2 content.

In a comprehensive exploration, T. Watanabe et al. [209] investigated the impact of boron doping concentration (1 at.% and 3 at.%) on diamond electrocatalysts using Density Function Theory (DFT) analysis. The study involved the scrutiny of four distinct models of boron doping on a diamond crystal through various configurations: (i) an isolated B atom located on a lattice site, (ii) pairs of boron atoms at the first nearest neighbor (1NN), (iii) pairs of boron atoms at the second nearest neighbor (2NN), and (iv) a tetrahedral arrangement of five boron atoms forming clusters. The analysis revealed that the 1NN model, illustrated in Fig. 24 (a-d), played a pivotal role in generating sp2 impurities (boron sp2 hybridization and carbon sp2 hybridization) in the diamond lattice, thereby influencing the electrochemical properties of the electrocatalyst. In the case of 1 at.% boron doping, the structure featured small densely packed diamond crystallites, and boron atoms were dispersed as single isolated substitutional atoms, leading to minimal sp2 content. Conversely, in 3 at.% boron doping, TEM analysis detected small voids and graphite phases partly along the grain boundaries. In addition, the doped boron atoms were observed to associate with neighboring boron pairs, resulting in the formation of sp2 hybridized carbon impurities. Fig. 24 (e) presents cyclic voltammograms in 0.1 M HClO4 after anodic oxidation with a scan rate of 100 mV/s, along with corresponding boron bonding in the diamond crystal lattice for both 1 at.% and 3 at.% BDD. This study highlighted the critical role of doping concentration in modulating the electrochemical properties of diamond electrodes and established a connection between electrode properties and the local structures of boron atoms. The findings shed light on the formation of undesirable sp2 hybridized boron or carbon atoms in the diamond lattice [209].

In the field of electrocatalysis for CO2 reduction, intrinsic defects play a crucial role in electrode performance. Y. Liu et al. [210] embarked on a study involving the preparation of N-doped nanodiamond coated on a 20.0 μm-high Si rod array (NDD/Si RA) at two different preparation temperatures (450 °C and 500 °C). The schematic illustration of the NDD/Si RA preparation is presented in Fig. 25a, with an inset showcasing SEM images of NDD/Si RA-450 °C. The NDD/Si RA (450 °C) demonstrated a low overpotential of −0.36 V for the reduction of CO2 to acetate, 1.65–1.9 times lower than that of NDD/Si RA (500 °C). This discrepancy was attributed to the larger number of N-sp3C species in NDD/Si RA (450 °C), as revealed by XPS analysis in Fig. 25b, acting as the primary active sites for CO2 reduction compared to N-sp2C species prevalent in NDD/Si RA (500 °C). Furthermore, an increase in N content from 0.93 to 2.12 at.% resulted in a significant rise in acetate and formate production. This study elucidated that N-sp3C was more conducive to ECRR than N-sp2C, and higher N-doping concentration enhances ECRR performance. Despite the inert nature of the Si substrate for ECRR, NDD/Si RA (450 °C) exhibited an impressive Faradaic efficiency of ∼77 % for acetate production at −1.0 V, accompanied by ∼14 % formate production. The efficiency and current density were also contingent on the electrolyte concentration of NaClO4, primarily providing conductivity, showing a linear correlation. Increased HCO3ˉ concentration led to a higher current density of CO2 reduction due to faster proton transfer. Acetate formation on NDD/Si RA (450 °C) followed the pathway: CO2 → CO2 •ˉ → (COO)2 • → CH3COOˉ, as depicted in Fig. 25d, demonstrating faster CC coupling than CO2 •ˉ protonation. Consequently, the high selectivity for C2 products in N-doped diamond electrodes arises from active N-sp3C species, elevated N content, high overpotential for H2 evolution reaction, and the larger electrode surface area provided by the aligned rod array structure, acting as an electron transport channel for CO2 reduction reactions [210].

In the context of electrode modification, the introduction of boron and nitrogen doping plays a pivotal role in dictating the selectivity of CO2 reduction reactions. Notably, B doping prominently led to the formation of C1 products, such as formaldehyde (HCHO) and formic acid (HCOOH), whereas N doping leans towards the dominance of C2 product formation, including acetic acid (CH3COOH) and acetate (CH3COOˉ). Intriguingly, the N and B co-doped diamond electrode (BND) exhibited a remarkable 93.2 % Faradaic efficiency in ethanol (CH3CH2OH) production. This outcome is attributed to the synergistic effect of N and B co-doping, fostering CC coupling reactions. The production rate of ethanol increased proportionally with higher N content, as validated by the generation of HCHO and HCOOH with 53.9 % and 26.1 % Faradaic efficiency on boron-doped diamond (BDD) in the absence of N doping. Conversely, within the N-doped diamond electrode (NDD), CH3COOH and HCOOH were produced with the Faradaic efficiency of 62.4 % and 24.7 %, respectively. However, these efficiencies were much lower compared to those of BND electrodes, which produced CH₃CH₂OH as the CO₂ reduction product with Faradaic efficiencies of 83.3 %, 88.8 %, and 93.2 % for BND electrodes synthesized with varying N₂ levels of 2.5 %, 5.0 %, and 10.0 %, respectively. Further insights from DFT calculations revealed that the diamond facet also plays a pivotal role in ECRR performance. The (111) facet was identified as more favorable than the (220) facet in the BND electrode for ethanol production due to its energetically favorable configuration. On the (111) facet of the BND electrode, the production of ethanol followed a comprehensive pathway: CO2 adsorption on the B atoms (*CO2) → *COOH → *CO → *COCO → *COCOH → *COCHOH → *COCH2OH → *CHOCH2OH → *CH2OCH2OH → CH3CH2OH. During this reduction process on the BND electrode surface, CO2 docked onto the B atom, forming a BO bond with CO2 (*CO2) in the initial step. Simultaneously, nitrogen facilitated *H transfer for hydrogenation reactions, such as *COCO → *COCOH, benefiting from a lower energy barrier for H transfer from the N atom (0.89 eV) [109]. This multifaceted understanding underscores the intricate interplay of dopants, electrode facets, and reaction pathways in tailoring the selectivity and efficiency of ECRR on diamond electrodes.

In addition to the influence of non-metal heteroatoms doping, the BDD electrode underwent modification through various palladium deposition times (30 s, 100 s, 300 s, 1000 s), investigating their impact on the Faradaic efficiency in CO production. As the deposition time increased, there was a proportional rise in the attachment of reduced CO2 species to the BDD electrode surface, leading to an escalation in Faradaic efficiency. Specifically, the Faradaic efficiency increased from 20 % in PdBDD30 (30 s deposition time) and PdBDD100 (100 s deposition time) electrodes to 30–50 % in electrodes with deposition times of 300 s, 500 s, and 1000 s. However, the maximum Faradaic efficiency was observed at the 300 s deposition time, followed by a decline in the 1000 s PdBDD electrode. This decline was attributed to the instability and agglomeration of Pd particles, causing detachment from the electrode surface and consequently reducing the current density for ECRR. Furthermore, the local environment during ECRR played a role in influencing Faradaic efficiency. Optimal stirring speed during the reaction was identified as a key factor in improving CO2 reduction, enhancing the mass transfer of CO2 to the electrode surface, and subsequently affecting Faradaic efficiency and selectivity. A comprehensive study conducted by P. Jiwanti et al. determined that a Pd deposition time of 300 s, coupled with a stirring speed of approximately 240 rpm, resulted in the highest efficiency for CO production, achieving an impressive 53.3 % Faradaic efficiency at −1.6 V [153]. This research sheds light on the nuanced interplay of deposition parameters and reaction conditions in tailoring the electrocatalytic performance of modified BDD electrodes for CO production.

Within the category of 3D carbon materials, particular attention has been drawn to carbon foams featuring macropores for their potential applications in ECRR. Carbon foams exhibit open-pore structures surrounded by carbon walls, interconnected through windows or openings. In a study by Z. Zhang et al. [211], diverse N-doped carbon foams were synthesized using template-assisted pyrolysis at variable temperatures to manipulate the types of nitrogen (N) functionalities—graphitic N (GN), pyridinic N (PN), pyrrolic N (ProN), and N-oxide (N-Ox). The different bonding configurations of these nitrogen types exert varying effects on charge distribution and polarization, consequently influencing the structural and electronic properties of the N-doped carbon foam (NCF). Upon analysis, the GN structure exhibited the highest Faradaic efficiency and selectivity for CO production, achieving 95 % at −0.5 V, and demonstrated stability for 80 h. In contrast, PN facilitated H2 evolution, while ProN favored both reactions at neighboring carbon sites. During the NCF preparation, an increase in pyrolysis temperature from 800 °C to 1100 °C resulted in the exclusive presence of the GN structure, accompanied by a significant decrease in the concentrations of PN and ProN. Moreover, CO production Faradaic efficiency increased from 25 % in NC-800 (enriched with PN and ProN) to 100 % in NC-1100 (enriched with GN), encompassing H2 generation. However, a further increase in pyrolysis temperature to 1300 °C led to a decrease in Faradaic efficiency due to the removal of N dopants from the carbon foam, acting as active sites for the reduction reaction, resulting in diminished performance. The distinct behavior of N-dopants in the carbon foam was attributed to variations in the electronic density of states of carbon modulated by these three different types of N dopants. In the GN-doped carbon active site, COOH* exhibited weak binding, as the density of states moderately decreased near the Fermi level, unlike PN and ProN, where COOH* intermediate demonstrated strong and weak adsorption, respectively [211]. As a result, the synthesis conditions for efficient carbon foams in ECRR applications need to be carefully optimized, considering their impact on electrical and physicochemical properties.

In a separate investigation, Z. Zhang et al. employed the SiO2 template pyrolysis method to synthesize a carbon foam incorporating single iron atoms (Fe-N4/CF-1000) through pyrolysis at 1000 °C [140]. This engineered material demonstrated significant promise for ECRR. The Fe-N4/CF-1000 exhibited a remarkable Faradaic efficiency of 94.9 % for CO production at −0.5 V. This high efficiency was attributed to the synergistic effects arising from the presence of pyridinic nitrogen and a substantial concentration of FeN4 moieties within the carbon foam. These configurations served as active sites for the CO2 reduction reaction by efficiently absorbing CO2 on the Fe atoms. Furthermore, the carbon foam's enriched macro-scale pores played a crucial role in enhancing electrical conductivity and facilitating the diffusion of reactants and products. Notably, the synthesized material exhibited impressive stability, maintaining its performance for over 60 h. This underscores the potential of the Fe-N4/CF-1000 as a robust electrocatalyst for prolonged ECRR applications [140].

S. Gao et al. [212] synthesized a PdBi bifunctional alloy anchored on a carbon substrate (BiPdC) via Pd doping in a Bi-based metal-organic framework (MOF) for ECRR, producing HCOOH as the primary reduction product. The rod-like Bi-based MOF was transformed into BiPdC through pyrolysis with PdCl2. The PdBi alloy, anchored on the carbon substrate, played a pivotal role in achieving a significantly higher Faradaic efficiency of 63.41 % at −0.97 V for HCOOH production compared to BiC (17.64 %) and 2BiPdC (49.5 %). Additionally, Pd doping in BiC suppressed the formation of H2 and CO, emphasizing the importance of Pd incorporation in enhancing ECRR performance. Notably, the BiPdC alloy exhibited bifunctional behavior, enabling reversible interconversion between CO2 and HCOOH [212]. Conductive carbon papers (CP) have also been employed as effective substrates for anchoring electrocatalysts due to their ability to support electroactive materials. J. Ding et al. [213] developed a porous Ag-coated electrocatalyst (p-Ag/CP) using SBA-15 as a template on the hydrophilic surface of commercial CP, with a geometric area of 1.0 × 1.0 cm2. The opposite hydrophobic side of the CP remained exposed to facilitate CO2 diffusion. This p-Ag/CP electrocatalyst demonstrated an impressive Faradaic efficiency of 90 % for CO production over a wide potential range (−0.8 V to −1.1 V vs. RHE), reaching a maximum of 96.7 % at −1.0 V vs. RHE in a 0.1 M KHCO3 electrolyte. In contrast, Ag/CP and bare CP exhibited efficiencies of 89.1 % at −1.0 V and 1.78 % at −1.2 V vs. RHE, respectively. The porous architecture of p-Ag/CP facilitated superior electron transfer, evidenced by its lower resistance (3.94 Ω) compared to Ag/CP (5.18 Ω) [213].

Three-dimensional carbon-based materials, such as porous carbon networks, offer hierarchical porous structures, high surface areas, and efficient ion transfer pathways, making them ideal platforms for anchoring electroactive materials. H. Han et al. [214] developed atomically dispersed iridium (Ir) species stably anchored on 3D porous carbon networks (Ad-Ir) through strong interactions between the Ir atoms and the carbon support. Among the synthesized materials, Ad-Ir-1.4 (containing 1.4 wt% Ir) exhibited the highest CO Faradaic efficiency of 95.6 % and an impressive turnover frequency of 33,365 h−1 at −0.62 V (vs. RHE), outperforming Ad-Ir-1.1 (1.1 wt% Ir), Ad-Ir-0.7 (0.7 wt% Ir), Ad-Ir-0.2 (0.2 wt% Ir), and Ir nanoparticles. The enhanced ECRR activity of Ad-Ir-1.4 was attributed to the atomically dispersed Ir species, which facilitated CO production by promoting CO2 adsorption, *COOH formation, stabilization of the CO2 radical anion, and subsequent reduction to CO. Furthermore, the high density of Ir species on the 3D porous carbon supports enhanced these processes. However, increasing the Ir loading beyond 1.4 wt% (e.g., Ad-Ir-1.6 with 1.6 wt% Ir) led to diminished ECRR performance due to the formation of Ir clusters, which reduced the availability of active sites and compromised the catalyst's efficiency [214]. H. Han et al. [215] synthesized nitrogen-doped porous carbon (NPC) decorated with 20 wt% copper (Cu/NPC), achieving high selectivity for the production of multi‑carbon alcohols, such as ethanol (C₂H₅OH) and propanol (C₃H₇OH). The product selectivity was tunable by varying the pyridinic nitrogen (N) content introduced into the porous carbon framework. Pyridinic N played a crucial role as a cocatalyst by enhancing CO₂ adsorption, with its content directly influencing the ECRR. The presence of pyridinic N species facilitated the formation and desorption of *CO intermediates due to their weak binding energy, as evidenced by CO production as a major product with the NPC electrocatalyst alone. When combined with Cu catalytic sites, the system produced multi‑carbon products via CC coupling reactions between *C₁ or *C₂ intermediates and *CO. This two-site mechanism utilized pyridinic N as the CO-producing site and Cu as the catalytic site, enhancing ECRR activity. Without N-doping, Cu/PC exhibited maximum Faradaic efficiencies of 30.2 % for C₂H₄ at −1.0 V (vs. RHE), 14.3 % for C₂H₅OH at −0.9 V (vs. RHE), and traces of C₃H₇OH at higher negative potentials. For Cu/NPC-800, optimized with specific N doping, the FE values significantly improved, achieving 64.6 % for C₂H₅OH and 8.7 % for C₃H₇OH at −1.05 V (vs. RHE). These results demonstrated that N doping in carbon materials and the density of pyridinic N content not only influenced product selectivity and CO₂ adsorption capacity but also affected CO production, electronic structure, and the size of Cu species, collectively enhancing ECRR performance [215].

K. Lv et al. [216] demonstrated the role of pyridinic nitrogen (N) species in facilitating the electrocatalytic conversion of CO₂ to multi‑carbon (C₂) products. In their study, silver (Ag) nanoparticles were anchored onto a 3D graphene-wrapped N-doped carbon foam (Ag-G-NCF), which served as a highly efficient catalyst for ethanol production. The Ag-G-NCF catalyst achieved a remarkable Faradaic efficiency of 82.1–85.2 % for ethanol formation within the potential range of −0.6 to −0.7 V (vs. RHE). The high catalytic activity was attributed to the synergistic effects of pyridinic N species and Ag nanoparticles. Pyridinic N species exhibited a strong binding affinity for *CO intermediates, stabilizing them on the catalyst surface. Simultaneously, Ag nanoparticles promoted the conversion of *CO intermediates into *OC–COH species, a crucial step in the reaction pathway leading to ethanol formation. This combination of functionalities effectively enhanced the selectivity and efficiency of CO₂ reduction to ethanol [216]. In a study on 3D carbon-based materials for ECRR, Y. Xing et al. [217] developed a 3D layered porous carbon nanotube aerogel as a support for lead nanoparticles (FD-PbNP/MWCNT) on carbon paper electrodes. The unique architecture of the 3D porous structure provided a large specific surface area, numerous active sites, and a highly conductive carbon network, all of which significantly enhanced the electrocatalyst's performance. As a result, the FD-PbNP/MWCNT catalyst demonstrated efficient CO₂ conversion to formate, achieving a Faradaic efficiency of approximately 84.6 % [217].
To provide a comprehensive overview, Table 5 summarizes the ECRR performance achieved with various types of 3D carbon-based materials employed as electrocatalysts.

Driven by the dual threat of rising CO2 concentrations and dwindling fossil fuel reserves, the electrochemical reduction of CO2 to valuable fuels has emerged as a critical research frontier for scientists, policymakers, and the public alike. This technology holds immense promise for establishing a sustainable energy landscape. Among the diverse roster of electrocatalyst materials, carbon-based materials are rapidly gaining traction for their unique properties. Their inherent environmental friendliness, affordability, and high surface area make them ideal candidates for ECRR. However, pristine carbon materials often exhibit limited activity or even complete inactivity towards CO2 reduction. To overcome this shortcoming, heteroatom doping has emerged as a powerful strategy to enhance ECRR performance. By strategically introducing heteroatoms like nitrogen or sulfur into the carbon lattice, we can modulate the electronic structure and introduce abundant electron density. This, in turn, significantly boosts the catalytic activity and selectivity of carbon-based materials, enabling the efficient conversion of CO2 into a range of high-value hydrocarbons and oxygenates, such as methane, ethylene, and ethanol. Therefore, heteroatom doping holds tremendous potential for unlocking the full potential of carbon-based catalysts in ECRR. Continued research and development in this area are crucial for advancing this transformative technology towards practical implementation, contributing to a more sustainable future through CO2 valorization.

Significant research findings have unveiled the catalytic ability of various zero-dimensional carbon-based materials. Notably, N-doped fullerene exhibited heightened current density and a more positive onset reduction potential compared to pristine fullerene. Maintaining an optimal sample preparation temperature, such as 800 °C for NC61, resulted in a remarkable 91.2 % Faradaic efficiency for formate production. This enhancement was attributed to the presence of substantial graphitic N-defects, serving as the primary active sites, and a larger electrochemically active surface area achieved through this temperature. Structural modifications to fullerene, such as fragmentation, introduced more active centers, surface curvature engineering, defects, and N and O doping. N doping predominantly yielded C1 products (formate, CO) with higher Faradaic efficiency, while Cu doping in zero-dimensional carbon-based materials led to C2 product formation (C2H4 and C2H5OH) due to a lower energy barrier for coupling *CHO and *CO compared to *CO dimerization. Beyond fullerene, Carbon Dots (Cdots) have proven effective as electron sinks in ECRR, enhancing both selectivity and activity. Doping in Cdots increases electron concentration on the catalyst surface, promoting CC couplings. Surface modification or incorporation of high amounts of oxygen-containing functional groups on the catalyst surface facilitates CO2 adsorption, reducing reaction, and CO desorption in ECRR. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), another zero-dimensional carbon-based material, primarily produce CH4 as CO2 reduction products. Fine-tuning GQDs' properties—such as shape, size, geometry, functionalization, heteroatom doping, and enriching with a high density of edge sites—can boost activity and product selectivity. Functionalizing GQDs with electron-donating groups favors CO2 reduction to CH4, whereas those with electron-withdrawing groups exhibit low CO2 reduction efficiency, favoring hydrogen evolution. A stronger electron-donating capability and a higher content of electron-donating groups, result in increased Faradaic efficiency and enhanced product selectivity. N-doping in GQDs, specifically the formation of pyridinic N at the edge sites, increases active site density and promotes CC bond formation, enhancing the generation of multi‑carbon C2 and C3 products in ECRR.

One-dimensional carbon nanotubes, while inherently inert, exhibit poor activity and selectivity in ECRR. Nonetheless, they find extensive use as supports due to their capacity to augment electrical conductivity, stability, and offer a highly porous surface area for reactants and intermediates adsorption. Nitrogen-doped CNTs (N-CNTs) emerge as catalysts with heightened activity, credited to the introduction of nitrogen defects. Among the nitrogen functionalities in N-CNTs—graphitic, pyridinic, and pyrrolic—graphitic and pyridinic N exhibit a positive correlation with catalytic effects by facilitating proton-electron pair transfers. On the other hand, pyrrolic N content follows an opposite trend. Predominantly, N-doping, especially pyridinic N, influences catalytic active sites in N-CNTs, resulting in high electrical conductivity, low free energy for CO2 activation, and weak CO binding. The presence of metal impurities introduces variations in current densities and product selectivity; Cu fosters CH4 production, while Ni promotes H2 generation, alongside small amounts of HCOOH and CO. Polymer coating on CNTs contributes to stabilizing active metals in the electrocatalyst, inhibiting degradation or aggregation. Another noteworthy one-dimensional carbon-based material, Graphene Nanoribbons (GNR), stands out as a promising support for electrocatalytic materials. GNRs offer structural and electronic tunability, mechanical flexibility, and high porosity. This unique set of properties enhances the interaction between electroactive nanoparticles and the support, thereby tuning catalytic stability and performance. Metallic doping in GNRs establishes a Mott-Schottky heterojunction interface, providing advantages for charge transfer. Semiconducting GNRs promote charge transfer and migration towards higher work function metal nanoparticles in the nanoparticle-semiconductor interface, effectively regulating the onset potential. Different configurations of GNRs, such as zig-zag and armchair edges, exert influence over selectivity, electronic structures, and catalytic efficiency in ECRR.

Two-dimensional graphene emerges as a versatile material for enhancing electron transfer and conductivity while serving as an anchoring platform for electroactive materials, including metals, non-metals, or molecular materials. Pristine graphene, however, faces challenges in binding to CO2 molecules, characterized by a substantial free energy barrier for *COOH intermediates formation. Doping and functionalization of pristine graphene introduce asymmetric charges on the surface, thereby modifying the electronic structure and providing a high density of active sites. The density of defects exhibits a positive correlation with catalytic activity. Increasing the concentration of N doping (graphitic and pyridinic N) correlates with decreased CO2 adsorption free energies. Thus, enhancing edge carbon sites and increasing graphitic N concentration can significantly improve ECRR performance. Dopant size also influences ECRR performance, with larger nanoparticle sizes limiting interactions and reducing the synergy between Cu nanoparticles and pyridinic N sites. Hence, optimizing the size and concentration of constituting materials is crucial for obtaining higher product selectivity and activity. N-doped graphene and single-atom catalysts show higher Faradaic efficiency, forming cyclam-like structures. The high surface area provided by the graphene support, coupled with the enhanced conductivity resulting from the interaction between pyrrolic N-doped graphene and metal atoms, enhances the ECRR process and overall performance. N-doped graphene materials are recognized as excellent support materials for electroactive substances such as metal nanoparticles, polymers, heteroatoms, oxides, etc., offering a large electroactive surface area for CO2 adsorption, good electron and proton conductivity, and an abundance of functional groups. Furthermore, the structural configuration or chirality of defective graphene, such as zigzag or armchair graphene, significantly influences ECRR performance by varying electron distribution and reducing binding energies of formed intermediates. The armchair edge carbon optimally distances metal atoms, facilitating interactions with CO2 molecules and promoting greater electron transfer. Moreover, in N-doped graphene, pyridinic N serves as a proton and CO2 absorber and activation site, facilitating hydrogenation and CC coupling reactions on active metal atoms. Therefore, intrinsic or extrinsic defects in carbon-based materials possess the capability to engineer the electronic and chemical properties of electrocatalysts, offering varying concentrations of electrocatalytic active sites and thereby impacting ECRR performance.

As a distinguished three-dimensional carbon allotrope, diamond typically exhibits insulating properties. However, the incorporation of dopants, introduction of defects, and inclusion of sp2 hybridization can effectively modify and tune the electrical properties of impure diamonds. In contrast to other carbon allotropes, which demonstrate superior ECRR activity upon nitrogen doping, researchers prefer employing boron doping to modulate the electronic properties of diamond. Boron-doped diamond (BDD) presents a broad potential window for operation, optical transparency, reduced fouling, and both microstructural and chemical stability. The use of BDD electrodes yields various C1 products, including CO, HCOOH, CH3OH, and HCHO. The flow rate of the electrolyte plays a crucial role in affecting the Faradaic efficiency as it modulates the mass transport of CO2 onto the BDD surface and the production rate of the products. BDDs demonstrate a high selectivity of 99 % for HCOOH production, as they exhibit limited binding capability for the HCOOH/HCOO− intermediate and effectively stabilize the CO2 •ˉ intermediate on the BDD surface. In this way, BDD electrodes tend to predominantly produce HCOOH as the primary product. The product selectivity and efficiency of BDD electrodes are also influenced by the type of electrodes used. Furthermore, the presence of sp2 carbon in the BDD electrocatalyst can alter the Faradaic efficiency and selectivity of the ECRR. In the presence of sp2 species, the production of CO and H2 becomes relatively higher than that of HCOOH due to the strengthened adsorption ability of sp2 surfaces for CO2 •ˉ intermediates. This phenomenon affects the selectivity of the ECRR by influencing the electronic structure and density of states.

In the exploration of N-doped diamond electrodes, the heightened selectivity for C2 products can be attributed to the presence of active N-sp3C species, increased nitrogen content, a high overpotential for the H2 evolution reaction, and an expansive electrode surface area facilitated by the structured alignment of rod arrays. With boron doping, the formation of C1 products such as HCHO and HCOOH predominates, whereas with nitrogen doping, C2 product formation, particularly CH3COOH and CH3COOˉ, becomes dominant. In the case of the N and B co-doped diamond electrode (BND), CH3CH2OH production is observed, showcasing the synergetic effect of N and B co-doping and the promotion of CC coupling reactions. Therefore, meticulous selection of carbon-based electrocatalyst materials, optimized dopant concentrations and types, electrolytes, sample preparation environments, and ECRR local environments are imperative for achieving higher product selectivity and Faradaic efficiency.

As we navigate through the fascinating realm of carbon-based electrocatalysts, this review propels us towards a future where precisely engineered materials hold the key to efficient and sustainable electrochemical CO2 reduction. The synergy between defects, dopants, and substrate structures unraveled herein sets the stage for a new era in electrocatalysis, where the rational design of carbon-based materials propels us closer to a greener and more sustainable energy landscape. Through this synthesis of knowledge, we embark on a transformative journey, ushering in a promising era of innovation and environmental responsibility in the pursuit of clean energy solutions.

Debika Devi Thongam: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Da-Ren Hang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Chi-Te Liang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Mitch M.C. Chou: Supervision, Resources.
